content.text:
Please analyze this codebase and create a CLAUDE.md file, which will be given to future instances of Claude Code to operate in this repository.

What to add:
1. Commands that will be commonly used, such as how to build, lint, and run tests. Include the necessary commands to develop in this codebase, such as how to run a single test.
2. High-level code architecture and structure so that future instances can be productive more quickly. Focus on the "big picture" architecture that requires reading multiple files to understand.

Usage notes:
- If there's already a CLAUDE.md, suggest improvements to it.
- When you make the initial CLAUDE.md, do not repeat yourself and do not include obvious instructions like "Provide helpful error messages to users", "Write unit tests for all new utilities", "Never include sensitive information (API keys, tokens) in code or commits".
- Avoid listing every component or file structure that can be easily discovered.
- Don't include generic development practices.
- If there are Cursor rules (in .cursor/rules/ or .cursorrules) or Copilot rules (in .github/copilot-instructions.md), make sure to include the important parts.
- If there is a README.md, make sure to include the important parts.
- Do not make up information such as "Common Development Tasks", "Tips for Development", "Support and Documentation" unless this is expressly included in other files that you read.
- Be sure to prefix the file with the following text:

```
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
```

content.text: This is a book manuscript repository, not a software project. It contains a single markdown file (`original_draft.md`) — a book draft about corporate governance by Abhik Pal.

input.content:
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Project Overview

This is a book manuscript repository. The book is titled "Avoid Misgovernance in Corporates" by Abhik Pal, a practical guide to corporate governance. The entire manuscript lives in `original_draft.md` (~649KB).

## Structure

- `original_draft.md` — The full book draft. Organized into 5 sections (Leadership, People, Operations, Support Services, Articles) across 30 parts, each containing multiple chapters.

## Working with the Manuscript

- The draft is a single large markdown file. When editing, use targeted edits rather than full rewrites.
- The book uses a consistent chapter naming convention: `Chapter: The "X" syndrome` for most leadership/operations chapters.
- Sections are delimited by bold markdown headers (`**SECTION N – TITLE**`), parts by (`**PART N: Title**`).

## Project Overview

This is a book manuscript repository. The book is titled "Avoid Misgovernance in Corporates" by Abhik Pal, a practical guide to corporate governance. The entire manuscript lives in `original_draft.md` (~649KB).

## Structure

- `original_draft.md` — The full book draft. Organized into 5 sections (Leadership, People, Operations, Support Services, Articles) across 30 parts, each containing multiple chapters.

## Working with the Manuscript

- The draft is a single large markdown file. When editing, use targeted edits rather than full rewrites.
- The book uses a consistent chapter naming convention: `Chapter: The "X" syndrome` for most leadership/operations chapters.
- Sections are delimited by bold markdown headers (`**SECTION N – TITLE**`), parts by (`**PART N: Title**`).

content.text: Created `CLAUDE.md` with an overview of the repository — it's a book manuscript, so the file focuses on the content structure and how to work with the large draft file effectively.

message.content: This is the v0 draft of a book written by my father in @original_draft.md , based on his experiences in climbing the corporate ladder within the management of a large multinational firm in India. I need this entire document to be proofread, correct all errors in spelling and grammar, as well as suggestions for better phrasing and sentence construction wherever applicable. Consider yourself as an expert editor and proofreader at a large publishing house, first break this down into markdown files for each individual chapter, git commit at this state, and then carry out the proofreading and editing for each chapter, and also create at the end a summary markdown document detailing all the edits and changes with respect to the original version, with proper references to locations in the original text. Use as many sub-agents as you need to carry out this big task in as parallel a manner as possible. Also, in the project, create a simple python script that allows us to easily one-shot convert all the markdown files into properly formatter pdf files.

   345→**INTRODUCTION TO THE BOOK**
   346→
   347→Thank you for showing interest to read this book. With all honesty I say that I am not a born or a known author. I am a retired industry leader with 36 years of experience in various corporates. I wanted to share my views on Corporate Misgovernance, the title of this book, particularly with management students and young professionals who aspire to be the leaders of future so that they can understand various avoidable decisions taken by leaders in the past and enable them to refrain from committing similar mistakes when they assume top positions in the corporate like Senior Managers, Departmental Heads, Business Unit (BU) & Strategic Business Unit (SBU) Heads, Deputy General Managers (DGM) , General Managers (GM), Assistant Vice Presidents (AVP), Vice Presidents (VP) or the Chief Executive Officers (CXOs) , Presidents & Directors.
   348→
   349→The chapters of the book discusses on several practical issues as I have seen, heard and understood. I hope you will enjoy reading the book.

350→
   351→**PREFACE**
   352→
   353→I am aware that there are thousands of books in the market on Leadership Management focusing on what Leaders should do illustrating different case studies. Though all these case studies may not be always appropriate in the Indian business environment or in the country where your organization is based, nonetheless they present very important issues handled by leaders, some of which may have had deep bearing on the viability, sustenance and profits of the organization. Leaders must learn lessons from these case studies so as to act appropriately in similar situations in future.
   354→
   355→However, this book revolves the other way round. **It focuses on what Leaders should avoid**. It cites numerous examples of wrong decisions taken by leaders, albeit in my opinion, some of which have led to serious consequences for the organization. As I grew up the ladder and eventually assumed a top position, I have come across various issues myself as well as those which I learnt from my friends who worked in different corporates at senior leadership positions. All these issues led to the compilation of this book which serves as a practical guide for young growing professionals aspiring to become leaders of future in their respective fields of expertise.
   356→
   357→Unlike many theories of management (meant for examinations?), I have narrated issues and cited decisions taken by leaders which were probably wrong in my opinion and led to various problems in the organization. In every such issue discussed, I have also tried to find out what other solution could have been more appropriate so that these problems were arrested in the first place itself.
   358→
   359→We are all human beings. Leaders too are. **“To err is human”.** People do make mistakes in life. So do leaders in organizations. Examples cited in the book are not meant to disrespect or demine any particular leader or refer to any particular individual, but rather to opine how they could have acted in a different manner. I am grateful to those same leaders belonging to various corporates from whom I have learnt so much for the growth and success in my own professional career. But, at the same time, I have analyzed how they could have taken decisions differently in many instances to avoid problems which cropped up subsequently. Some may disagree with my opinions or the solutions mentioned, nevertheless, it will provide them some food for thought on how they would like to act under such circumstances if arising in their future.
   360→
   361→The entire book has been broadly grouped into five Sections and each Section into different Parts. Each Part deals with issues of a particular domain of operations or department. Each issue of the particular domain is discussed under different chapters constituting the Part. The role of the department is first explained in the Part and thereafter each chapter against this domain discusses on a particular issue and the problems aroused and the likely solution. Some issues discussed may seem simple (not to all) and some chapters are small, may be a one pager, but I felt that such “simple or one pager” issues are worth discussing because they are important to an organization. Remember what Shakespeare said:  **“All small incidents in life together create a huge impact on our minds”.** Similarly many small decisions in the corporate may create huge impact on the organization over time. And hence the importance of recognizing issues, big or small. Some of you may already be aware of some of the issues cited and they may feel that these issues are a commonplace not worth mentioning. However, I have sometimes observed that leaders still take such inappropriate decisions and therefore these issues deserve mention and discussion for the benefit of all.
   362→
   363→Almost all the chapters of the book are in reference to construction projects in the Indian context since construction projects encompasses different domains of management. All the chapters are purposefully hilariously titled to draw your attention on the core issue. 
   364→
   365→At the end of each Part, I have also listed some good practices which may be adopted by leaders to create a **“feel good factor”,** a proper corporate governance model and a good working environment amongst employees in the organization. It’s up to you to adopt should you feel it as relevant. 
   366→
   367→In Part 26 of this book, I have explored some new concepts which come across my mind. I leave it to your wisdom to adopt them or ignore. Annexures in Part 27 of the book illustrates some concepts for your better understanding. Part 28 provide some personal advices which are interrelated between your personal and corporate life. These issues are often ignored while we are busy at work in office but they affect your life, family, work, work-life balance and your finances and most importantly your health \- mental, physical and spiritual. Hence it’s importance. Part 29 narrates some lessons I learnt in the corporate which helped me to get along with people at different challenging times.
   368→
   369→As a SBU Head, I had the privilege to work with my BU Heads, Departmental Heads of Strategy, Marketing & Business Development, Advertising, Designs & Engineering, Bidding & Costing, Project Management, Site Operations, Production Management, Supply Chain Management, Fleet and Equipment Management, Stores Management, Quality, Safety, Human Resources, Administration, Contracts & Law, Finance & Accounts, Audit, Information Technology, Learning and Development, Insurance, Ethics, ESG, CSR and learnt the varied dynamics of different domains of operations. These experiences gave me a detailed insight to taking right decisions and helped me to flag different issues discussed in the book.  
   370→   
   371→Right from beginning of my career, I had an inclination to analyze different decisions taken by leaders in various corporates under various circumstances. Such observations were of great interest to me. As I grew up with more experience, I felt that many of these decisions taken could have been different and I always wondered on what could have been done right. After I retired, I thought that the only way I can convey my thoughts on right governance to young professionals and management students was to write this book.

   424→
   425→**SECTION 1 – LEADERSHIP**
   426→
   427→**PART 1**
   428→
   429→**CXO/SBU/BU/DEPARTMENT HEAD LEVEL LEADERSHIP**
   430→
   431→**The role of Leaders in a corporate**  
   432→First and foremost, remember, Leadership does not necessarily always mean a top position like the President or Director of an organization. Even the In-Charge of the Staff in a big organization is a Leader who drives all the day-to-day staff activities. The Site Manager is also a Leader under whom various positions in the project site work for the successful completion of a project. Essentially, a Leader means someone who leads his team in all the activities that the team is responsible for.
   433→
   434→However, the weightages of top leadership is much higher since his actions will have a higher and huge impact on the organization. The weightages of the departmental heads as senior leaders is definitely less than that of the Managing Director, but nonetheless it is also very important for achievement of the goals of the organization.
   435→
   436→A Leader has multifaceted role in a corporate as narrated below:
   437→
   438→1. Visionary planning – setting a clear direction for the organization in the domain he is responsible for  
   439→2. Strategic decision – evaluating the market and developing techniques which will drive to achieve his goals  
   440→3. Corporate culture – actions in line with the cultural values of the organization  
   441→4. Stakeholder relationship – building relationship with stakeholders like employees, vendors and customers  
   442→5. Risk management – identifying potential risks and developing strategies to mitigate them  
   443→6. Leadership management – develop juniors to leaders of future   
   444→7. Finance management – monitor financial health in his domain of work
   445→
   446→Let us understand the structure of an organization and its role. An organization is usually spearheaded and driven by the Board of Directors (BoD). It forms the **Vision and Mission** of the organization and layouts a structure and foundation on which the organization treads its path to meet its goals and objectives. BoD comprises of top leaders from its own company or group companies. Now-a-days it’s mandatory to include a Director from an independent company, called the Independent Director, who would evaluate the performance of the organization from a third angle perspective. A BoD also comprises of a Lady Director who can analyse a company’s performance and activities from her perspective. The BoD may have several Directors on its Board and is led by the Chairman of the company. The Managing Director is also a part of the BoD reporting to the Chairman. The Managing Director drives the organisation in line with the Vision & Mission set by the BoD.
   447→
   448→A V**ision** statement details where the organization aspires to go. Why does your company exist? What do you hope to accomplish in the next several years? These are what a Vision addresses. But a M**ission** statement defines the **organization**’s business, its objectives, and how it will reach these objectives. The entire organization must drive their actions in line with the Vision and Mission statements of their organization.
   449→
   450→The Managing Director has under him Executive Directors and various CXOs to perform vital functions of the organisation. Each Executive Director may be working with some of the CXOs. For example, the Chief Financial Officer (CFO) looks after all the financial activities of the organisation. The Chief Strategy Officer (CSO) is responsible for finalising the strategies of different businesses of the organization, and so on. Essentially, the Managing Director and below are fully active in leading all the actions of an organization. The Chairman and the BoD may not be full time active, but reviews the organizational performance and decisions from time to time.
   451→
   452→An organisation is generally structured into different **Strategic Business Units (SBUs)** catering to different businesses of the organisation. One SBU may be involved with power projects, another SBU with Infrastructure projects like the airports, townships, etc., another SBU with Chemicals, so on and so forth. Each SBU is headed by a SBU Head who reports to a CEO (one of the CXOs). Each CXO like the CEO, CSO and CFO has a team of its own and works with each Strategic Business Unit (SBU) in that business domain. Like this, there can be several CXOs in the organisation who would report to the Executive Director or the Managing Director.
   453→
   454→Each SBU may be subdivided into different **Business Units (BUs)**. For example, the Infrastructure SBU may have a BU for airports, another BU for townships, another BU for sea-ports, etc. Each BU is headed by a BU Head and reports to the SBU Head.
   455→
   456→Each SBU may have several supporting Departments to support the different services that its BUs requires for its performance. Each such Department is driven by a Head (Department Head) who works for the BUs and may have dual reporting system, reporting to the SBU Head administratively and to the concerned CXO functionally. Examples of these support departments are Human Resources (HR), Administration, Contracts & Law, Finance & Accounts (F\&A), Audit, Information Technology (IT), Learning and Development (L\&D),  Insurance, Ethics, ESG and CSR
   457→
   458→The Chairman, Managing Director, EDs, CXOs and SBU Heads usually form the Top Management level. The BU Heads and the Departmental Heads usually form the Middle Management Levels. The Managers and Officers below the Departmental Heads usually form the Junior Management level.  
   459→The **Board of Directors (BoD)** is responsible for making key strategic decisions that determine the organization’s long-term direction, governance framework, and overall oversight.  
   460→**Top Management** translates these decisions into clear strategies and actionable plans, providing leadership, direction, and accountability across the organization.  
   461→**Middle Management** ensures coordination and alignment between strategy and operations, facilitating communication, resource deployment, and smooth execution across departments and business units.  
   462→**Junior Management** carries out the day-to-day operational activities, implementing plans, delivering results, and ensuring that organizational objectives are achieved effectively and efficiently.  
   463→To summarise, The BOD decides and frames the Vision & Mission of the organisation; The Top Management provides the **Direction** guided by the Vision and Mission of the BOD: The Middle Management is responsible for the **Facilitation** to the Junior Management for seamless performance: The Junior Management is responsible for the **Implementation** of the goals of the organisation. **Decision, Direction, Facilitation and Implementation** are the  top-down process for performance in an organisation.
   464→
   465→All references and examples of leaders in the book usually refer to these Top and Middle Management Leaders whose decisions impact the organisation significantly.
   466→

500→
   501→  			**CHAPTER : THE “TOO MANY CXO” SYNDROME**
   502→
   503→Let us see how many CXOs could possibly and theoretically fit in an organisation. The Departments the CXO may head are mentioned within brackets.
   504→
   505→CAO – Chief Administrative Officer (Administration)  
   506→CBO – Chief Business Development Officer (Business Development)  
   507→CCO – Chief Customer Officer (Business Development)  
   508→CDO – Chief Design Officer (Designs & Engineering)  
   509→CEO – Chief Executive Officer (Operations)  
   510→CFO – Chief Finance Officer (Finance)  
   511→CGO – Chief Governance Officer (Audit)  
   512→CHO – Chief Human Resources Officer (HR)  
   513→CIO – Chief Information Technology Officer (Information Technology)  
   514→CJO – Chief of Joint Operations (Operations)  
   515→CKO – Chief Knowledge Officer (Learning & Development)  
   516→CLO – Chief Learning Officer (Learning & Development)  
   517→CMO – Chief Marketing Officer (Marketing)  
   518→CNO – Chief Negotiation Officer (Supply Chain Management)  
   519→COO – Chief Operating Officer (Operations)  
   520→CPO – Chief Procurement Officer (Supply Chain Management)  
   521→CQO – Chief Quality Officer (Quality)  
   522→CRO – Chief Redresser Officer (HR)  
   523→CSO – Chief Strategy Officer (Strategy)  
   524→CTO – Chief Technology Officer (Operations)  
   525→CUO – Chief Unifying Officer (HR)  
   526→CVO – Chief Vigilance Officer (Governance)  
   527→CWO – Chief Wellness Officer (HR)  
   528→CXO – Chief eXperience Officer (Strategy)  
   529→CYO – Chief Youth Officer (Administration)  
   530→CZO – Chief Zone Officer (Operations)
   531→
   532→Some names sound funny, isn’t it? That was just to have fun and complete the series from A to Z. You can also name them as you wish\! Now let us come to the main point. 
   533→
   534→Sometimes it is observed that the Top Leader tends to recruit several CXOs thinking that it will help in different activities of the organisation seamlessly and the Top Leader can be relatively free from interventions in all the activities of the Departments. However, the problems of recruiting many CXOs are as follows:
   535→
   536→1\. The Top becomes heavy and the Employee Overheads increases, as a result of which the company becomes less competitive in the market. The Top Management must be the lightest economically in an organisation. Their total salaries and number in an organisation must be restricted to avoid a Top Heavy Organisation. Their individual salaries may be high but, just to provide an idea, for a large organization, their total salaries could be around 10% of the company’s Cost to Company (CTC) and numbers restricted to around 1% of company’s total employee strength. 
   537→
   538→The Middle Level is kept heavier and the Junior Level is kept the heaviest, both in terms of total salary and numbers. Junior Level is the implementing level, hence the heaviest. However, I have observed too many CXOs being recruited by some companies making them top heavy.
   539→
   540→2\. Too many CXOs sometimes provide their own ideas which are contradicting to each other. The leaders below CXOs get confused faced with different directions from the different CXOs. For example, a CFO instructs the international Bidding team not to bid with less than 7% profit margins anywhere abroad, but the CBO asks the same team to bid with Zero profits in a particular country as entry costs to establish as a first time entrant and subsequently increase their presence. The irony is the CFO and CBO may be reluctant to discuss the issue amongst them. 
   541→
   542→3\. Often the CXOs are egoistic and some resort to politics to prove their superiority. They try to create an atmosphere down below that their directions are the most superior and need to be followed even if not suggested by other CXOs. This creates confusion down below as to whose directions need to be followed. In the above example, the CFO might try to impose his directions on the bidding team, rather than take up the matter with the CBO with the intervention of MD and form a consensus.
   543→
   544→4\. Each CXO wants to recruit and strengthen his team in the corporate office though their concerned Departments in various SBUs may have sufficient number of people of similar expertise. For example, the Finance Department of various SBUs may already have a Chartered Accountant (CA), however the CFO may intend to recruit another CA under his belt. Also these recruits, being under CXO and stationed at corporate office, try to flex their muscles to employees of the concerned Departments. This creates a non-cooperation environment between the department and the CXO’s team.
   545→
   546→**“Too many CXO”** becomes a common chant amongst employees when they find one after another CXOs being unnecessarily recruited. 
   547→
   548→The problem is that all departments need a CXO to be monitored and directed. But how many is the question. There are simple solutions to this problem. 
   549→
   550→1. Top Leaders need to combine related functions together to reduce the number of CXO recruits. With lesser number of CXOs, there will be better unity, harmony and clarity of directions in the organisation. For example, Business Development & Marketing, Advertising, Bidding & Costing and Customer Relations can be under the CBO. Finance, Accounts and Audit can be under CFO. Administration, HR, Learning & Development, Wellness department, Unifying Officers, and Governance can be under CHRO. 
   551→
   552→2. Under each CXO, there can be different expertise in his corporate team or in the Departments to look after different activities. For example, under CHRO, there could be different Managers either in his corporate team or in the department to look after Administration, HR, Learning & Development, Wellness, and Unifying activities but CHRO remains as the overall single Chief. Also, under CFO, there could be one Chartered Accountant (CA) in his corporate team and it may not be unnecessarily duplicated by recruiting another CA in the concerned Department.  
   553→     
   554→3. Also, different CXOs should meet together to form a consensus for providing same directions to the Department or SBU Heads. For example, the CSO, CFO and the CBO must align together to decide what profit levels the Bidding team should consider to enter an international market for the first time. The profit margin to be considered could be very less if there is huge competition in that market. It could be negative, in other words, bid with anticipated loss to be sure to win, if the market is very big and the presence and learning of the market during operations is of utmost importance. It could be high if the competition is scarce and past results show that the contractors generally bid with high profit margins. There could be different scenarios, but the essence is the CSO, CBO and the CFO must form a common opinion and guide the Department in one direction. 
   555→
   556→Let me give you another example. Imagine the pandemonium that would be created if the Cricket Control Board thinks to provide best services in international cricket by recruiting a batting coach, a spin bowling coach, a fast bowling coach, a fielding coach, a wicket keeping coach, etc. Instead there can be a single coach who has a broad knowledge of all the areas of the game. In bowling itself, there are different kinds of bowlers like the off spinners, leg spinners, fast bowling, swingers, googly bowlers, etc. but that does not mean that the Board has to recruit different coaches for different kind of bowling. In batting too, some are proficient in hitting sixes, some in cover drives, and some in sweeping. All these batting abilities require different capabilities, however, irrespective of all these variations, the coach needs to be one or maximum at two who can provide all round required expertise to all the players. This would enable to provide all the players a uniform direction.
   557→
   558→Similarly pandemonium will be created if in football there are different coaches like the free kick coach, dribbling coach, penalty shootout coach, goalkeeping coach, mid fielder coach. Isn’t it?
   559→
   560→So, in an organisation, **the CXO strength must be kept to a minimum** and the various related activities of the organisation can be clubbed together under a single CXO, as illustrated above.
   561→
   562→If you become a Managing Director someday, I wish you be so, please carefully study the business model of your organization thoroughly and discuss with key people to layout your structure of CXOs. Modify them if possible if the structure does not suit with your thoughts or align with the business you are in. Provide each CXO clear roles and responsibilities having no conflict amongst each other. Organise meetings between concerned CXOs if they have different views so that a common final view can be cascaded down below to avoid confusion and provide clarity.
   563→
   564→**The salient points are:**
   565→
   566→1. Limit your number of CXO recruits  
   567→2. Limit CXO salaries as a maximum percentage of company salary  
   568→3. Engage relevant CXOs to form a consensus on strategic decisions  
   569→4. Eliminate redundancy in positions amongst Departments & CXO’s office.
   570→
   571→**CHAPTER : THE “RENOWNED GUY” SYNDROME**
   572→
   573→Right person for the right job\! Readers may think what new I am saying. Yes, nothing new. Yet I cannot but state its importance again and again because I have seen organisations faltering in this area.
   574→
   575→I have observed that often very renowned personnel are recruited for important positions but eventually they have been found to be non-performers. Why? Simply because the recruit is not the expertise in the particular field even if he is renowned by name. You have to question what are you actually renowned for?
   576→
   577→For example, with due respect, Sachin Tendulkar was one of the renowned cricket players of the world but he would have probably performed badly if he was made the Hockey captain of India. Similarly Pele or Maradona were renowned footballers on earth, but they were unlikely to have performed as the Cricket captain of Brazil or Argentina. Am I correct? A Gynecologist and a Cardiologist both may have a MBBS degree and may be renowned, but you cannot expect a Gynecologist to perform a Heart Operation. Such blunders do happen in the corporate. Someone in the industry may be very renowned in Marketing but he cannot perform well if he is put into Operations. Sometimes HR gets swayed away on a person being so renowned, but the question is whether he is the right person for the right job that you are looking for?
   578→
   579→Mistakes are committed in another aspect also. Suppose you interview a Procurement officer working in a small sized SBU of a large sized company. The particular SBU may be dealing with few vendors on a small scale of annual procurements. But you get swayed away by the fact that the Procurement officer works in a very large company and thereafter provide him the position of Chief Procurement Officer in your company where you need to deal with hundreds of vendors on a large scale of annual procurements. Can he perform? Definitely not. It’s like asking the pilot of a small propeller jet to fly an A380 Airbus. Imagine the likely fate of the passengers\!
   580→
   581→Recruitment in senior positions must consider the following:
   582→
   583→1\. Understand from various sources about a new recruit on his field of expertise, the volume he deals with and the number of employees who report to him. Many candidates fake experience. A Project Manager may cite Bidding experience to try out for a senior post in Marketing. The HR must verify the credentials and actual experience. The scale of operations handled by the candidate is also to be determined. Customers and vendors may be consulted to get ideas about the candidate. Do not get swayed away just because he may be renowned in the industry, or he may be someone belonging to a large organization of repute but actually handling small scale operations.
   584→
   585→2\. HR needs to be careful about the qualifications versus experience issue. I will not prefer a highly qualified person having average skills and experience in small sized corporates; rather I would recruit a moderately qualified person having much greater experience in bigger corporates. Qualifications do count a lot. The IIT, IIM and other reputed institutional brands do count, but not at the cost of experience. I have great respect for well qualified professionals, but I have found many mediocre qualified employees performing better than them, sheerly because of better and wider experience. You may not know that most Directors of different corporates were not the best qualified engineers with the highest marks. They were definitely reasonably well qualified but with great acumen and experience to lead organisations. You may have scored the highest in your Law degree course, but you may not necessarily be the best Lawyer on earth\! Therefore while HR must consider qualifications for recruits, gauging their experience in the particular field is also extremely important.
   586→
   587→3\. Another critical point. Experience does not necessarily mean the number of years of experience after your college degrees; it is more about the talent and capability you have earned to deal with critical issues during your experience. There are middle level professionals who can deal with critical problems in a far better manner than a senior professional. Well, the question then comes, how did he at all become a senior professional? The answer could be two fold. In the earlier days, seniority was achieved by the number of years you spent after your college degrees and you may have risen based on total years of experience or you may have risen through unethical support by your unethical and biased Leaders. The point is, count on the talent and potential of the candidate rather than concentrating on his total experience. 
   588→
   589→You will find a chapter dedicated to this later titled “Grow by KNOWLEDGE, not by AGE”. Today, corporates hunt talent; they do not necessarily hunt seniority.
   590→
   591→**The salient points are:**
   592→
   593→1. Recruit right expertise for the right job, not look at qualifications alone  
   594→2. Renown does not find a place, the right expertise has  
   595→3. Total experience does not count, what counts is the talent, capability and potential  
   596→
   597→**CHAPTER : THE “OIL CHAIN” SYNDROME**
   598→
   599→Senior Leaders must recruit other junior leaders on their merit, talent, potential and credentials. Same should be the principle while evaluating existing leaders. But sometimes senior leaders fall prey to their juniors who have expertise to find ways to make their bosses happy (this process is called oiling) through unethical means.
   600→
   601→What is oiling? Hahaha\! Everyone knows about it. But still let me try to explain it in context to corporates. Any unethical means to keep one’s boss or seniors happy is called oiling. The irony is some bosses like it. Oiling may include saying “yes sir” to everything that the boss says even if it is not right, always tells boss how great a leader he is though in reality he is not, ignores his duty and assists the boss in his personal works to please him, doing some household chores for the boss’s family, gifting the boss or his family expensive gifts, arranging parties for boss/family at his own cost, so on and so forth.
   602→
   603→An “oil chain” means a chain of leaders oiling up the ladder. Say a Department Head oils his boss who is the Business Unit Head, the Business Unit Head oils his boss who is the SBU Head, the SBU Head oils the COO, the COO oils the ED and the ED oils the MD and so on. This is an example of “Oil Chain”. The chain can be long or short depending on the chain of personalities involved. If a leader is straight forward, honest, ethical and unbiased, the oil chain stops at his subordinate level. However, this subordinate may oil the boss of his boss so that his boss gets eliminated in the chain.
   604→
   605→People are involved in the oil chain mainly to seek undue favours like promotion for himself or someone else, increments, bonus, favours in work sphere, relief from punishments in case of an offence, undue facilities like international travel, office car use, etc.  
   606→
   607→Resorting to oiling by these elements in the organization creates mistrust amongst honest employees who toil hard for the organization; they feel cheated. These honest employees may even seek to resign and try for better prospects in other good organisations. So an organisation may lose some of their talent in the process. It is said that most, may be 80%, leave organisations because of their bosses, not because of the organization itself. Therefore a boss needs to be honest and unbiased and refrain himself from being oiled.  
   608→
   609→Every leader is recruited by his higher ups at some point or the other. The HR or the senior leader in charge of such recruitments must be careful to check the **personality and sincerity of the individual**, other than his qualifications, experience, knowledge, skills and talent. Right and honest personalities keep an organisation moving from strength to strength ensuring harmony and equality amongst people. An organisation cannot afford to lose honest and talented individuals just because of their unethical bosses. Dishonest, oiling and oiled leaders cannot take an organization to great heights.
   610→
   611→Once I had an interesting discussion with one of my Executive Directors as to why many government and private organisations look into performance of individuals in Classes X and XII. What do such results mean in a corporate? The answer was very interesting.
   612→
   613→It is said that a person attains his personality traits by the age of 18\. People may be able to change their personalities thereafter but only to a certain small extent. The bulk of the characteristics of a human being are attained normally by 18 years of age. Therefore good marks scored in Classes X and XII indicate the past sincerity of the individual. If his results were good in Class X (aged approx. 16 at the time) and in class XII (aged approx. 18 at the time), it is commonly assumed that the person is sincere even now. This may not be true at all times; there are insincere people today who were sincere in their early lives and also vice-versa. However, looking into school and college results may give some idea about the sincerity of people. And it is assumed by an organisation that such sincere students would continue to work sincerely in the corporates.
   614→
   615→The moot point here is that the importance of one’s personality and sincerity of an individual cannot be over emphasised. The HR Head must observe or get feedback from deprived employees of any biased decisions taken by their leaders in the appraisal process. Oil chains significantly disturbs the appraisal process, which means, often the right candidates are not rightly rewarded. If required for senior leaders, HR can take a 360 degree feedback from those who report to the seniors with regard to their performance and appraisals. Oil chains must be eliminated to ensure that good talent prosper in the organization. Let us summarise the possible solutions;
   616→
   617→1. HR must recruit or leaders must appraise based on merit (qualifications and experience), talent, potential and credentials of employees ignoring the oil chain, if any.  
   618→     
   619→2. HR must try to ascertain the personality and character of an individual so that oil chains do not flourish.  
   620→3. HR may keep a good watch during appraisal process where the oiled leaders tend to give good appraisals to underserving employees.
   621→
   622→4. HR must also note the achievement of individuals from time to time so that they can also contribute positively during the appraisal process.  
   623→     
   624→
   625→**The salient points are:**
   626→
   627→1. Appraise based on merit, talent, potential and credentials  
   628→2. Discourage oiling and break oil chains  
   629→3. Personality, honesty and sincerity of an individual matters, not only his qualifications, experience, knowledge, skills and talent  
   630→4. If bosses display true unbiased leadership, talent can be retained
   631→
   632→**CHAPTER : THE “LETHARGIC AND REACTIVE” SYNDROME**
   633→
   634→Have you read the book “The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People”. The first chapter itself deals with the habit of being proactive. 
   635→
   636→Please understand that some of your competitors, may not be all, are always on the lookout for opportunities. So when an organisation comes across an opportunity, it should be quick to act upon. Or if the CSO has come up with a good Strategy, the organization must be quick to implement the same. 
   637→
   638→Once at a point of time, an organization had ample opportunity to bag construction projects in an African country. But there were lot to study and understand to avail the opportunities like the labour and tax laws of the country, the infrastructure available, the anticipated profit levels, the vendors and subcontractors available, the award system, the repatriation laws, the organization’s manpower required in the country, the office location, overall costs, etc.
   639→
   640→Every analysis for the above started but very slowly. It was more than a year that above analysis was completed. By the time, one competitor did their due diligence fast and was quick to secure an order too. This organization was late. When an organization enters a new market for the first time with the presence of only a few competitors, you can expect good profit margins. But as time goes by, more competitors come in, competition increases, chances to win decreases, and profit margins dwindles even if you win. 
   641→
   642→In the life time of a particular business, whether the business is related to a product or a service or a project where products and services are integrated, you will find that the profit margins during the early period of the lifetime is high and thereafter it diminishes to a point when running the business makes no sense. That is why you need to attempt and grab opportunities at the earliest. If the opportunities require lot of due diligence before you dive into those, you need to study and complete your due diligence and take necessary actions very fast so that you can thereafter try to avail of those opportunities. The sooner, the better, is the mantra. 
   643→
   644→Also an organization must be innovative to produce new products or a service or find new methods and new technologies to implement a project. However, even a new product or service will have a lifetime with the phenomenon of diminishing returns over a period of time. So the constant endeavor is to constantly change with constantly high speed. It could be a change in the market, change in technology, change in the product or service, etc. Constant change and upgrading an organization is the key to success but such changes have to be fast in order to grab the opportunities with optimum profits.
   645→
   646→In another example, an organization had lot of business opportunities in some countries. But the problem was the prices to be quoted were fixed. The projects involved many raw materials for supplies where there were fluctuations in the cost of raw materials over time. Also the bid prices to be quoted were in dollars whereas the supporting vendors quoting to the organization were in INR (Indian Rupees). So there was currency fluctuation risk too involved in the bid price. To be safe from such fluctuations, the organization knew that there were hedging processes available in banks which charged a premium for the coverage. But the organization did not know the entire process of hedging and the cost thereof. Studies started but at a very slow pace. During this time, the competitors, who were already experienced in the hedging process, grabbed lot of opportunities with good profits.
   647→
   648→Therefore the faster you grab an opportunity, the better returns and also the number of opportunities you get. You must be proactive all the time. That’s the essence. Don’t be lethargic and reactive and then go to a saturated market after your competitors have grabbed the initial opportunities. The CSO, CBO and the SBU Head must act very fast and request other CXO or Department Heads to be proactive to learn the required new aspects of an opportunistic market and dive into the market as soon as possible to reap the maximum benefits. 
   649→

1000→1. Communication is an art to be mastered by all leaders  
  1001→2. Proper communication can arrest rumours and grape vine  
  1002→3. Communication provides clarity to employees and eradicates confusion  
  1003→4. Communication is a medium to share good ideas across organisation
  1004→
  1005→**CHAPTER : THE “MISUSE” SYNDROME**
  1006→
  1007→Some Leaders are habitually unethical and irresponsible spenders of money of the company. 
  1008→
  1009→Once a new ED was recruited in an organisation. Several EDs and Directors in the past worked successfully in the existing office but this ED thought that his office needs a grand makeover. The building also housed hundreds of employees of different levels. Upon proposal and convincing the Board, a hefty sum was approved and a complete renovation was carried out. The exchequer was quite high. After a few months, the President thought that the company’s Head Office should have been in another big city, incidentally where his home town was located. This too was approved by the Board and a new office was taken where all the top leaders of the company were relocated. The earlier President and the newly renovated ED’s office remained vacant as a waste while the other employees continued working in the same building in other floors as they were earlier. 
  1010→
  1011→Several questions come to one’s mind. Was such renovation of the ED’s existing office required? Was relocation of Top Management Office to a swanky office elsewhere required? Why was not this relocation of office thought before so that the renovation of ED’s office could have been avoided? Why, after a short time after renovation did thought come across to relocate to a bigger city? Are these expenses worthwhile with respect to the financials of the company? Did the earlier ED or Directors face so much of problems of space or inconvenience that they could not work properly? Could such renovation or part relocation wait for some more time? Can the relocated top leaders have meaningful coordination with the existing office now where most of their employees are still housed? Did not the Board think of all these issues while they provided their approval? Isn’t it therefore that such a renovation or relocation was a waste of company’s money?
  1012→
  1013→In another instance, customer’s representative comes to your office at around 11 am for a meeting. The leader craves for a good lunch. It’s quiet sometime that he did not savour a good buffet lunch in a five star hotel\! So, what does the Leader do? He unnecessarily hosts a lunch for the guest in a five star hotel after the meeting just to satisfy himself for the lunch even if the guests were not keen for the lunch. Very cunningly, the leader takes along with him a close subordinate for the lunch so that the subordinate signs off the bill (the leader being not the spender\!) who then sends the bill to the same leader for approval. The leader signs the bill. Alas\! Rs 25,000 gone from the coffers of the company simply for the fact that the leader actually wanted a good lunch, not the guests\!  
  1014→
  1015→Sometimes, to maintain cordial relationship, hosting a lunch or a dinner for a stakeholder is nothing wrong, but to do it numerous times to satisfy your own greed for good food at the cost of the company is unethical and waste of company’s money.
  1016→
  1017→Accounts Department needs to be vigilant on such avoidable expenses which are actually personal in nature but made official like I mentioned above. 
  1018→
  1019→Once there was a marriage of a Director’s daughter of a big client. The Director extended invitation to the Leader personally and not to others in the company. Hence this was purely a personal invitation. However, the Leader convinces his Management and Accounts that the invitation is to the company and therefore a gift needs to be presented on behalf of the company. The Management, upon trust, assumes that the invitation has been extended to some top leaders of the SBU and thereby approves the cost for an expensive gift. However, when the gift is handed over, it is done in a manner as if the gift has been presented by the leader in his personal capacity. 
  1020→
  1021→In all such cases where actually the leader was supposed to spend money ethically from his own pocket, he disguises every time as if the incidents are related to the company and thereafter spends the money from the coffers of the company. A leader is supposedly paid well; still he hankers after company’s money for personal expenses. It is an irony that such well to do people in a company are hungry to grab more and more facilities unethically and make or save more money irrespective of the wealth they may have already amassed. 
  1022→
  1023→Whether you want to make or save money, that’s entirely up to you, but definitely you cannot trick a company to pay for your personal events from their coffers\!  
  1024→
  1025→**The salient points are:**
  1026→
  1027→1. Expenses of a company must be optimized and regulated  
  1028→2. Leaders must refrain from spending company’s expenses for their own vested interests  
  1029→3. HR and Accounts must be vigilant to stop unnecessary spending from the company’s coffers by dishonest leaders
  1030→
  1031→**CHAPTER : THE “BEST PROJECT AWARD” SYNDROME**
  1032→
  1033→The best executed project at site is the most deserving project for an award for a leader, but unfortunately the leader makes mistakes in such determination. Site execution cannot be the only criteria to be judged as the best executed project without taking cognisance of how and at what costs the project was financially closed. Therefore a Project should wait for its financial closure to be determined for award because rework during warranty period might reduce the profitability as determined after the physical completion of the project. Projects competing for awards must be financially closed first. Projects physically completed and commissioned but not financially closed should not be considered for award.
  1034→
  1035→Many management students in B-schools are not aware of what are all the terms of a financial closure of a project. Briefly, the same are as follows.
  1036→
  1037→1\. Physical completion of the project  
  1038→2\. Commissioning of the project, i.e. say for an electric transmission project, the successful commencement of transmission of electricity  
  1039→3\. Complete reconciliation of all billings billed to the client for the project and the payments received by the contractor  
  1040→4\. Providing Retention Bank Guarantee  
  1041→5\. Replacements and rework, if necessary, during warranty period at contractor’s cost  
  1042→6\. Successful completion of warranty period.  
  1043→7\. Retrieval of all bank guarantees from the client.
  1044→
  1045→As illustrated in a later chapter on the Project Health Card, the project parameters to be considered for the best project award are as follows:
  1046→
  1047→1\. Construction Time  
  1048→2\. Direct & Indirect costs, overheads, finance costs, Total costs w.r.t. budget  
  1049→3\. Profitability w.r.t Construction Time after considering rework and replacements during warranty period  
  1050→4\. Use of Technology  
  1051→5\. Quality of construction  
  1052→6\. Safety methods implemented during construction  
  1053→7\. Ethical standards  
  1054→8\. Sustainability implementation  
  1055→9\. CSR project undertaken   
  1056→10\. Cash Flow of the project  
  1057→11\. Financial closure of project
  1058→
  1059→The SBU Head should provide a weightage to each of the above parameters for each project closed financially and thereafter judge which project is the best FINANCIALLY closed project. Normally the leader tend to judge the best project based on the top three parameters as illustrated above, which is wrong.
  1060→
  1061→Use of Technology ensures better quality of work. Quality of construction ensures longer durability of the project. Safety methods ensure safety and wellbeing of human lives. Ethical standards raise the name and fame of an organisation. Sustainability measures indicate caring for your environment and the future of our earth. Cash Flow determines the requirement of capital for the project. And financial closure frees the organisation from all financial obligations of the project. Hence the other eight parameters too deserve significant weightage.
  1062→
  1063→Suppose a project calls for huge cash capital for execution of the project but yield average profits. The capital requirement of the project may be so big that it may have disturbed the cash flow and timely completion of other projects. Then the question is whether such an order was worth for the company? Can such a financially closed project involving huge capital and yielding average profits be considered for award even if it was executed well? Thus determining the best project for award is a critical judgment based on all the parameters discussed and their associated weightages.
  1064→
  1065→Often the leaders consider a particular project as the best without deep diving on all the parameters of a good executed and financially closed project. Best executed project is also sometimes chosen based on the leader’s relationship with the Project Manager. Such malpractices will dissolve if there is a fair mechanism to judge the best project.
  1066→
  1067→**The salient points are:**
  1068→
  1069→1. The best financially closed project is the best executed project  
  1070→2. Various parameters of Financial closure must be taken into account for evaluating the best project   
  1071→3. Award must not be given to best physically completed project but given to best financially closed project which also considers physical completion
  1072→
  1073→**CHAPTER : THE “BID LOSS BLAME” SYNDROME**
  1074→
  1075→In a bid, various components of costs are considered by the Bidding team of an organisation. The Design Head provides design data based on which costs are evaluated. The Supply Chain Head provides best quotes of vendors. The Finance Head provides the best financing and bank guarantee costs. The Project Management Head provides the best Site management and overhead costs. The HR Head provides the salaries of different grades of Project and Site manpower. The Advertisement Head provides the annual advertising costs to be appropriated and considered in each bid. The Equipment Management Head provides the cost of using equipment and machinery for a project, and so on and so forth.
  1076→
  1077→The Bidding Head is responsible to collate all information, documents and costs from various departments and compile the price bid to be submitted to the client. So far as costs are considered, he discusses and negotiates with all departmental Heads to get the best competitive costs to be incorporated in the bid. Of course the Bidding Head also considers the market, competition, likely quote of competitors, importance of winning the project to determine the profit levels to be considered before finalizing the price of the bid.
  1078→
  1079→Some costs mentioned above have a major impact in the bid cost, and some costs have low impact. For example, the vendor costs have a major impact in the bid cost since supplies may take up 70% of the total bid cost.
  1080→
  1081→People should understand that Bidding & Marketing department provides only the Marketing costs of the company to be incorporated as overhead costs in the bid. All other costs are from other different Departmental Heads. The main job of the Bidding team and the Head is to put a proper documentation of all techno-commercial requirements as required and specified in the bidding documents (Part – 1: Techno-commercial bid) and the Price Schedules of different Supplies and Services required for the Project (Part – 2: Price bid)
  1082→
  1083→Now here is the problem. Whenever a bid is lost, the Director often pounces on the Bidding Head as if he was the main culprit for losing the bid. All other Departmental Heads like the Finance Head, HR Head, SCM Head, etc. are scot free and takes no accountability for the loss even if the quotes given by them during bid submission were actually not competitive. It is shameful that the Bidding Head is often the scape goat and the poor chap has to bear the brunt of bid losses. Such a thought process needs to change in the industry. In fact, I will discuss in more details in a subsequent chapter, that one of the evaluation criteria in the Annual Appraisals **of all Departments** of an organisation that must be incorporated is the percentage of bid win. The weightage of such criteria across different Departments may vary depending on the cost impact of the Departmental cost towards bid cost. Unfortunately, the weightage for percentage of bids won in all other Departments is not considered as a KRA in their Appraisals, as if no other Department other than the Marketing & Bidding Department was responsible for bid losses. This is a very unfortunate phenomenon existing in organisations. Various Departments must be responsible for bid losses to the extent of their cost impact towards the bid. Similarly, they must be rewarded for bid wins to the extent of their cost impacts. It is also not correct that the bidding team takes full credit of bid wins when other departments contributed to the bid cost leading to winning bids.
  1084→
  1085→Particularly, the major costs of a bid are the vendors which may take up 65% to 80% of total bid cost and the Project management costs at site which may take up 20% to 30% of the bid cost. Hence the SCM Head responsible for vendor costs and the PM Head responsible for Project Management costs are the major cost impact departments. Other departments may impact around 10% of bid costs which cannot be ignored because even a non-competitive cost offer from this 10% impact zone may lead to a bid loss by a thin margin. 
  1086→
  1087→Also there is a question of a bad win and a good win. This aspect will be discussed in a separate chapter.
  1088→
  1089→Therefore, when a bid is lost, a Director may question not only the Bidding Head but also the SCM Head and the PM or Operations Head. The Bidding Head cannot be the only scape goat. 
  1090→
  1091→**The salient points are:**
  1092→
  1093→1. A Price bid has to consider costs given by different Departments  
  1094→2. The SCM and the Project Management Departments are the bulk of the costs in a Price bid  
  1095→3. The Bidding Department develops only the Marketing Costs on their own, all other costs are provided by other Departments  
  1096→4. The Bidding Head alone cannot be responsible for bid wins and losses  
  1097→5. Appraisals must provide appropriate weightage to all Departments for bid wins and losses based on their cost impact in the Price bid 
  1098→
  1099→**CHAPTER : THE “WILD DREAMS” SYNDROME**

2000→
  2001→**CHAPTER : HAVING LION’S EYE, NOT AN EAGLE’S**
  2002→
  2003→Do you know how many hours does a lion sleep? It’s around 18 to 20 hours. The balance time in a day is for hunting an eating. So a lion is basically a very lazy animal.
  2004→
  2005→Also do you know the power of the eyesight of an eagle? Its expertise in picking up its moving target on ground is highly impressive, just like a kingfisher picks up a fish from a river within seconds.
  2006→
  2007→Why am I talking about all these animal instincts? I will tell you why in just a while.
  2008→
  2009→A tender comprises of techno commercial and financial specifications, may run into thousands of pages which a bidder has to carefully go through and prepare his bid. Ignoring any important line or clause in the specifications may cost the organisation dearly. There are a multiple examples of this. I will cite some.
  2010→
  2011→The first example is that of mine. When I was the BD Manager, a tender for construction of a particular Transmission Line was announced. I prepared a good techno commercial and price bid and submitted the same to the client. Later on I was shocked to know that our bid was rejected. One of the lines amongst thousands of pages of specifications read that the bidder must be a manufacturer of tower structures, which we were not at that point of time. I somehow missed the line while I read the voluminous bidding documents. And this cost the organisation dearly because had our bid opened we would have been L1 and secured the order.  
  2012→   
  2013→In another example, there were two tenders related to the same project, the first tender for the first geographical portion of the project and the second one for the next geographical portion of the same project, say Tender 1 and Tender 2\. To maintain secrecy of the final bid price,   
  2014→the Head asked the junior engineers to prepare bid prices with escalated rates for all items, the intention being that he will submit a discount vide a discount letter and attach with the bid to arrive at the final bid price. The Head wanted to attach a 5% discount letter for Tender 1 and a 15% discount letter for Tender 2 to arrive at his intended final bid prices. The Head then did a massive blunder. He put the 15% discount letter in Tender 1 and the 5% discount letter in Tender 2\. So what happened is that the Tender 1 submitted became undervalued (ie. would incur loss when executed) and Tender 2 became overvalued. The result was that the organisation won the contract against Tender 1 and lost lot of money after it was executed. And Tender 2 being overvalued was lost. Remember, after a bid is submitted or awarded, you cannot rectify your mistakes. That is the rule of bids and awards.
  2015→
  2016→Another example was very interesting. The specifications said that the bids must be submitted on so and so date by 9.30 am. The bid had to be submitted on the third floor of the client’s office building. Many bidders submitted their bid by 9.30 am; however it was astonishing to find that a very competitive bidder had not submitted its bid by that time. Many bidders were happy at the thought that the competition would be less. However, all of a sudden from nowhere, this competitive bidder came running and submitted his bid at 9.35 am. All other bidders represented that this late bid should not be accepted by the client. However, this bidder represented that he had entered the client’s office and registered his attendance at 9.28 am but then he waited for the lift in the building and it was 9.35 am by the time he could submit the bid. Amidst this confusion, the client immediately instituted a committee of directors and finally the committee directed that the bid be accepted since the bidder had registered his attendance in the office of reception of the client at 9.28 am, thereby his bid will not be considered as late. 
  2017→
  2018→The English of the specifications can sometimes be confusing leading to rejection of bids. For example, one specification stated “the contractor must have experience in three similar transmission line projects of minimum 100 kms which involved supply of conductors and insulators in the project”. Now there can be different interpretations like this:
  2019→
  2020→1. Do all the three projects required supply of conductors and insulators both?  
  2021→2. Does it mean that one project could have supplied insulators only and the other two supplied conductors?  
  2022→3. What happens if one of transmission line had no supply of conductors or insulators but the other two had?  
  2023→4. Is it that each of the transmission line should have been 100 kms long?  
  2024→5. Is it that the cumulative length of all the transmission lines should be atleast 100 kms long?
  2025→
  2026→Sometimes the English used in specifications can be distorted and differently interpreted by different bidders. In such cases, it is best to raise clarifications to the client in writing and act accordingly. 
  2027→
  2028→Numerous such interesting examples can be given. But in essence what I am trying to drive at is that the Bidding & Costing Department must keep their eyes open and has to be as sharp as that of an eagle’s. You cannot be lazy like a lion and miss the details of a bid. In all cases of blunders cited above, they were cases of oversight. Heads and employees of Bidding & Costing cannot be excused for such oversights because you lose lot of opportunities for the organisation and at times lose lot of money for such oversights.
  2029→
  2030→Because of my oversight in the first case, I was reprimanded. However, it was a big learning for me to be extremely careful thereafter and always read specifications with an eagle’s eye. I never used to read specifications if I was stressed or not feeling like to read at any point of time.
  2031→
  2032→HR must ensure that Heads and managers in Bidding & Costing are very meticulous in reading specifications without oversight. You must be an eagle, not a lion.
  2033→
  2034→**The salient points of concern are:**
  2035→
  2036→1. A bidding engineer must be meticulous in studying the bid documents  
  2037→2. Submission of documents for different tenders must be carefully packaged so as to not mix documents amongst tenders  
  2038→3. If a clause arise multiple interpretations, ask clarifications in writing to the client
  2039→
  2040→**CHAPTER : BEING THE RODENT, NOT THE OWL**
  2041→
  2042→The rodent is known as one of the most impatient animal in the world. Every second it wants to move in search of food. On the contrary the owl is the most rare patient spirit animal and creature. In case you encounter an owl, the universe is giving you a sign. This animal represents wisdom, patience, solitude, deep connection and intuitive knowledge.  
  2043→   
  2044→In the department of Bidding & Costing, one needs prolonged concentration and patience to go through the huge bidding documents minutely keeping a note of all the important clauses and terms of the specifications based on which the techno-commercial bid and the price bid would be prepared.
  2045→
  2046→There are men who are often distracted during work for different reasons including time off for smoking. There are women who are also often distracted for various reasons including time off for facial touch ups. I am not discouraging either of them, but what I am trying to mean is that people in this department needs to have deep concentration for prolonged periods before breaks so that they do not miss the minute details of the documents. It has been observed that employees resorting to regular breaks often miss some important points of the documents, particularly at the point they left reading before the break and commence reading after the break.   
  2047→   
  2048→Once, someone took a break while being on the middle of a chapter while going through a technical document. After the break, he resumed browsing the specifications, but missed a paragraph while doing so. The paragraph contained an important product parameter to be considered in the bid, which was ignored during the preparation of the bid. It’s a simple guess for what happened after the submission of the bid. The bid was technically rejected.
  2049→
  2050→In another instance, I was anxious about someone’s health while I was reading a specification requirement that the bidder has to be a manufacturer for a particular product. I did not understand its importance probably due to lack of concentration and I did not make this a point to be considered in the bid. Eventually the bid was rejected.  
  2051→
  2052→So specifications have to be read minutely in order not to miss any points in any chapter. It helps to make your technical and commercial bid responsive. HR and top leaders must ensure that other than the requisites of an employee to work in the Bidding department, prolonged patience to go through huge bidding documents is a must. 
  2053→
  2054→You must display the characteristics of an owl being patient and concentrating on every move, not the rodent being distracted here and there every moment.  
  2055→
  2056→**The salient points of concern are:**
  2057→
  2058→1. A bidding engineer must have ample patience to read voluminous bidding documents.  
  2059→2. A bidding engineer must love reading bidding documents. If you do not like reading after a while, you are bound to miss some important points.  
  2060→3. Many engineers do not have the power of concentration. They will not be a good fit in the bidding department. This is not to say that they are bad, they may be fit well in some other departments.  
  2061→
  2062→**CHAPTER : DIVULGENCE FOR GAINS**
  2063→
  2064→Bidding and Costing is a sensitive department. Every detail needs to be kept secret because you are competing with the bids of others. So any information leaked from your bid to your competitor may prove to be very dangerous leading to loss of bids.
  2065→
  2066→There are some scrupulous people who take advantage of this. They pass on secret information of bid and costs to other competitors in lieu of unethical money. These people are dishonest and are not loyal to their own company. They know that they can earn good money by such unethical means though their company may not be able to win tenders. If situation comes that the company is struggling for existence due to continuous loss of bids, they would switch over to some other companies and resort to malpractices there also.
  2067→
  2068→Bidding team members must keep a thorough and a minute watch if such malpractice is adopted by any team member. HR and the Vigilance team must also keep a close watch. Bid opening results for certain bids may also provide a clue if something of this sort is happening in the department. The Marketing Head should monitor which bid has been prepared by which engineer leading to bid losses despite preparing competitive bids. If required, employees may be interrogated for foul play.
  2069→
  2070→It may so happen that other department employees roam freely in the bidding department and get hold of certain crucial data which can be passed on to a competitor for vested interest. Therefore this department must have a separate glass enclosure within the office premises with permission to access by other department employees if need be.
  2071→
  2072→**The salient points of concern are:**
  2073→
  2074→1. The Marketing Head, HR, Vigilance team and bidding team members must be vigilant if any data is being leaked outside by any employee for personal gains.  
  2075→2. The bidding area must have a separate enclosure with locked doors making it inaccessible to any other department. Permission to access the bidding department may be on need basis.
  2076→
  2077→**CHAPTER : MALPRACTICE OF MISCOSTING**
  2078→
  2079→Winning a bid against competitors is a tough job. A decent strike rate for winning tenders could be between 10% and 15% where there are a number of competitors in the fray.
  2080→
  2081→Sometimes a incapable bidding engineer, though might have been recruited based on interviews earlier, fails to prepare competitive bids. So they resort to malpractice as follows.
  2082→
  2083→In a complicated bid like that for an EPC project, there are thousands of cost data to be analysed and derived. For example, for a simple item like concreting a floor slab of a building, you need to know the costs of cement, sand, coarse aggregates, reinforcement of different sizes, wires to bind the reinforcement, transportation costs of all these materials from their sources to the nearest point for construction, head-loading cost of these materials from the nearest road point to the actual site, cost of shuttering, placement of reinforcement, mixing concrete, usage of equipment like mixer, vibrator, labour costs, etc.
  2084→
  2085→Similarly, there are hundreds of items in a complicated project where thousands of data has to be analysed. It is not possible for a Head to leave all his work and check all these data analysed by the bidding engineer. Taking advantage of this, a unethical engineer, in order to try to win a bid, may intentionally consider lesser cost of materials than it is so in reality, and make the bid competitive, however rendering it un-executable financially. The bidding engineer may be further cunning enough to manipulate these costs of items which would actually be executed at the end of the project execution. For example, the electrical and sanitary fittings are executed at the end of the project execution.
  2086→
  2087→The advantage is that the bidding engineer gets sufficient time to plan his course of action before getting caught. Bid evaluation of a project may take 4 to 6 months, another month for award, designs and engineering may take 4 to 6 months of time, and thereafter the item of construction where the bidding engineer may have manipulated would come up after another year. So it’s almost two years that the manipulated item of construction is taken up. Therefore the engineer gets two years time before the company understands that the items have been under quoted. Two years is a long time by which the engineer would have mal-practiced by under quoting in many bids. Many such bids would have been awarded and during this time of two years the engineer may have been congratulated, facilitated, awarded and promoted with good bonuses\! Then when the time comes after two years before he is exposed, he switches over to other companies in case he is accused for such under quoting or carries on in the company if no punishment is given to him.
  2088→
  2089→The Head, by virtue of his position, must be aware of such malpractices. He has estimates of different costs because of his experience. So he should look into the final costs, instead of going into the details for which time would not be available) that engineer has prepared before finalising the bid. If the Head gives time to overview the costing before a bid is submitted, he can easily detect if under quoting has been resorted to.
  2090→
  2091→Also after a bid is won, the Project manager assigned for the construction of the project after award may go through all the item costs right from the beginning to end to understand if the costing of all the items have been done rightly.
  2092→
  2093→**The salient points of concern are:**
  2094→
  2095→1. Some bidding engineer may resort to under quote some items of the bid intentionally to be lower price and win bids.  
  2096→2. Such bids when awarded are actually un-executable financially.  
  2097→3. Marketing Head must browse all item costs with his experience before the bid is submitted to understand if any malpractice has been adopted.  
  2098→4. The assigned Project Manager, on bid becoming L1, can validate cost of all items. However, any malpractice of under quote found at this stage will put the bidder in financial crisis since the bidder cannot change prices at this point of time.  
  2099→
  2100→**CHAPTER : THE EXTRA TIME AND VALIDTY PROBLEM**
  2101→
  2102→Sometimes upon request of the bidder in order to complete a bid preparation or because of the requirement of a client, the bid submission time is extended. The bidder in the meantime may have received offers from vendors which are valid for the required period of time as per the original specification. For example, say a bid was supposed to be submitted on 1st January of any year with a bid validity of 3 months, ie. Valid till the end of March 31st; but the time of bid submission is extended to 15th January by the client with the same bid terms, then the different mistakes that the bidder often overlooks are the following.
  2103→
  2104→1. The vendors and subcontractors may have submitted their offers with validity till 31st March. But with the extension of time of bid submission by 15 days; the offers required validity till 15th April in line with client’s terms. But the same might have been ignored by the bidder. Now if the award is given by the client say on 10th April, then the offers of the vendors at this time of award is not valid. The vendor can rightly say that their offer is not valid if it finds that the offer terms and prices are not appropriate during this period of time due to cost escalations. The vendor has every right to change its prices and terms after its original validity and therefore the bidder is in deep trouble.
  2105→
  2106→2. Another mistake that a bidder does is that, even if the time for bid submission is not extended by the client, the bidder does not ask for a validity of offer from their vendors and subcontractors for at least one month more beyond the validity of the bid. This practice should be resorted to because if the client places the order on 31st March (ie. Within bid validity period), thereafter the SCM department may take some time, say a month, to negotiate and finalize the order with the vendor. Now if the offer validity of the vendor was till March 31st, then the same problem crops up as cited above.  
  2107→   
  2108→
  2109→However, there is an important point to note. Sometimes a client allows variable prices of items with respect to time based on a given formula. If such formulas really take care of the cost escalation over time, then there is nothing to worry. But if the bid was quoted with firm prices and the bid is extended based on client’s requirement, then there is a cause of worry in case the cost escalations are beyond the fixed prices quoted. In such a case, asking for more validity from vendors makes sense.  
  2110→Another important point to note is that after the bid is submitted and the bid is L1, and thereafter the client has procedural delays and intends to award beyond the validity period, the client will ask the bidder to extend his bid validity. In such a case, the bidder has every right not to extend his bid if it finds that it cannot sustain the cost escalations taken place over this period of time until it is compensated by the price variation formula of the client. 
  2111→
  2112→The irony is if the bidder does not wish to extend the validity of its bid after the bid is submitted which it has every legal right to do so, the client is often angry and tries to convince the bidder to extend its bid. This is because the client knows you have given the best price and therefore needs your extension of validity of the bid. Also usually, the client requires three valid techno-commercial responsive bids in order to award a bidder. 
  2113→
  2114→**The salient points of concern are:**
  2115→
  2116→1. Ask for one more month of validity from the vendors and subcontractors than that required in the bid so that SCM gets one month to negotiate and place orders in case the client awards the project at the fag end of the validity period of the bid.   
  2117→   2. Ensure that the vendors and contractors do not quote higher because you have asked for one month of validity of their offers.  
  2118→      3. As and when the client extends the bid validity before the bids are submitted, the bidder should ask similar extensions of offer validities from the vendors and the contractors.  
  2119→         4. In case of bid validity extensions before bid is submitted, bidders must evaluate cost escalations vis-a-vis the price variation formula the client has stipulated in the bid. In case of fixed price bids, bidders need to carefully evaluate based on extensions of validity given by their vendors.
  2120→
  2121→**CHAPTER : THE COMPLETION TIME ISSUE**
  2122→
