  2123→In tender documents, the completion period required for executing a project is stipulated. The contractor may be executing many such similar projects at different sites. The Bid Manager may notice that the time taken to execute such projects or similar projects under execution is far more than the time stipulated in the current bid pertaining to a similar project.
  2124→
  2125→The Bid Manager consults with the Project Manager and understands that the Project Management costs in the bid must be considered for some more months than stipulated in the bid due to anticipated longer duration for completion. However, this will result in higher cost and therefore the bid may become uncompetitive. Even with the stipulated period of completion, the Bid Manager could consider “Crashing” of the project, in which case again the bid becomes uncompetitive.
  2126→
  2127→Sometimes in such cases there arises a conflict between the Bid Manager and the Project Manager. The Bid Manager, to become competitive, intends to consider the stipulated completion period for the Project Management Costs asking the Project team to become more efficient henceforth, whereas the Project Manager wants that the Bid Manager consider some more months for project completion because he anticipates a longer duration for project execution. And sometimes they quarrel and fight\!\!\!
  2128→
  2129→The above scenario happens in many organisations. The basic problem here is that the Project team thinks that winning bids is not his responsibility and his KRA, promotions and increment shall be irrespective of bids won or lost by the Bidding team. Therefore, in one of my chapters, I have raised a serious issue; is only the Bidding team responsible for winning or losing bids? Or, equally responsible are the Designs cell, the Project cell, the Finance cell who provides key data affecting the overall bid cost?
  2130→
  2131→The Project team also fails to note one very important aspect of business. If a Bidding team does not win bids, there will be no projects to be executed by the Project team. An organization generates turnover and profits and thereby provides salaries only when projects are executed. Without project execution, none gets salary including the Project team. And without bidding competitively, the organization does not win projects. It is therefore in the interest of all employees of all departments and the organization that everyone support the Bidding team to be competitive. To do so, all departments must find means to be more efficient. Project Team may also need to find means to be more effective and complete projects in stipulated period of time so that the bidding team does not unnecessarily consider higher Project management costs and become uncompetitive.
  2132→
  2133→In today’s world, cost of inefficiency cannot be considered in bid. If you are inefficient and escalate costs because of your efficiency, you will cease to be a player in the market. Either you be efficient or perish in the market. Let not a bid become uncompetitive because of inefficiency of Project team or any other department who contribute significantly to bid costs.
  2134→
  2135→**The salient points of concern are:**
  2136→
  2137→1. Since bidding team have to put their most competitive bid, Project team should not insist Bidding team to consider more months of project execution than stipulated in the bid until and unless it is really practically not feasible.  
  2138→   2. Project team and all other departments must find efficient means to provide their most competitive costs to the bidding team for them to consider in their bids.  
  2139→      3. In today’s competitive environment, cost of inefficiency cannot be considered in a bid. If you do so, you will cease to be in the industry.
  2140→
  2141→  
  2142→
  2143→**CHAPTER : THE ONLY CULPRIT FOR LOSING BIDS**
  2144→
  2145→The Marketing or the Bidding Department of an organisation is reprimanded for failure to secure orders. Yes, they are the final decider of the price bids submitted by them, hence reprimanded for losses. But the story should not end here.
  2146→
  2147→There are several costs built up in a bid. Manpower costs are primarily driven by HR. The more optimised, effective and experienced manpower are recruited by HR, the more the bid cost will be optimised. Supply Chain (SC) Costs is another major cost built up in a bid. The SCM Department is responsible for evaluating and recommending the best responsive yet cost competitive vendors in the bid. Failing to do so will jack up bid prices. Finance is another significant contributor to bid cost. Finance Department must have the best negotiated fianance costs with partner banks so that the cash flow finance costs built up in a bid are as less as possible. The overhead costs of the organisation must be reasonable. Often it is found that the top heavy management increases the overheads and therefore the bid cost. Logistics cost provided by the Fleet Department should also be optimised. The right technology and equipment to be used during the project execution as determined by the Technology Department also has a reasonable share in the bid cost. And very importantly, it is the Designs Department who provide design data based on which product and service costs are determined and considered in a bid. Bad designs alone can render a bid highly uncompetitive in spite of other factors being optimised. The Marketing team too provides the proportionate cost of marketing and advertising in each bid.
  2148→
  2149→Therefore, I find it amusing that during appraisals, the Bidding Department is either facilitated or punished alone for securing or not being able to secure orders. The Bidding team actually compiles costs obtained from various departments and forms a bid just like a music director composes the singing with other instruments to form a song. However, since the bidding team is responsible to have a market intelligence of probable winning levels, its role to negotiate and accept costs from all other departments cannot be under estimated. At the same time, other departments who provide and contribute to bid costs cannot be ignored and be scot free for bid losses.

4500→Also, don’t miss your annual or bi-annual basic health check-ups comprising of blood tests, Chest x-ray, USG and ECG. It is interesting to note that many people think they are fine and healthy but health check-ups reveal that certain health parameters are beyond normal values which require immediate treatment. What I am trying to sensitise that many such illness develop within our bodies SILENTLY.  For example, thousands of people do not know that they are pre-diabetic or have high blood pressure because they don’t check. These are silent killers. So please don’t miss your health check-ups.    
  4501→**Health is wealth. It is one of the most precious assets that one possesses. Please don’t ignore**  
  4502→   
  4503→**PORTFOLIO MANAGEMENT**
  4504→
  4505→This is again a ignored subject while we are in service. Someone comes and convinces of investing in a FD, we do it. Someone comes and asks to invest in ULIPs, we do it. Someone else comes and convinces us that there is lot of money to be earned in the stock market, we invest in the equities. Someone says to buy a flat instead of paying rents, we do so. That someone could be you yourself. You may think to put some money in gold, you do it. Whatever someone tries to convince or we are convinced about investment, we do it. However, all these investments are done in a haphazard manner.
  4506→
  4507→Here comes the issue of portfolio management. There is no harm in investing money in different class of assets, but it has to be done in a scientific manner. Firstly, the class of assets can be classified into equity, debt, property and metal. The percentage of investment into these different class could be based on one's age (the less the age, the more may be invested in equities), risk taking power (the less the risk taking power is, the more may be invested in debts), interest (if on a transferable job, someone may not be interested in property). 
  4508→
  4509→A typical mid aged person with moderate risk taking power can invest like;  
  4510→35% on equity  
  4511→25% on debt  
  4512→25% on property  
  4513→15% on metal
  4514→
  4515→Equity could be the stock market or equity MFs. Debt could be bank FD, corporate FD and corporate bonds. Property could be residential or commercial. Metal could be physical gold or silver, paper gold or silver, platinum or copper, zinc and aluminum in the MCX stock exchange. 
  4516→
  4517→Please take the help of a portfolio manager, who could be your Finance guy himself, to guide you for a good portfolio.
  4518→
  4519→**Invest wisely to maintain a good portfolio which will lead to earning good profits**
  4520→
  4521→**WORK-LIFE BALANCE**
  4522→
  4523→This is a hot debate often happening even on TV debates. How many live s have God given you? One, I suppose. Therefore you are never going to get the time lapsed of your life. Even the richest man on earth cannot buy time which has lapsed. Time gone is gone forever. And your past is also gone forever.
  4524→
  4525→So, while we have to struggle very hard in the corporates to do well in life and rise up the corporate ladder, we also need to give time for yourself (me time) and your family and friends. All is not work in life. We definitely have to work; it is a foundation to have a great life with our family and friends. So you need to balance between work and life and that is what is called the Work-Life balance. If you provide all the time to work, your relationship with your family may not be so intimate and loving, you may lose your friends and you may end up leaving all your hobbies that you so much loved to pursue.
  4526→
  4527→For a few days the balance may be upset due to pressure of work or on family matters, but on an overall basis you must strike a balance between work and life.
  4528→
  4529→**WORK-LIFE balance is about providing time to office as well as to our near and dear ones.**
  4530→
  4531→**GRATEFULNESS**
  4532→
  4533→Why do we touch the feet of our parents when we meet them? Why do we touch the feet of our beloved school teachers or our coaches? Because we are grateful to them for the care, love and teachings they have imparted to us. Without their care and support, we would not have been what we are; therefore they have been large contributors to our success in life.
  4534→
  4535→However, very unfortunately, I have seen many people not being grateful to their contributors once the contributor is no more in a position to contribute to you anymore. For example, as a business Head, you might have taught the intricacies of business to your subordinate and encouraged him and promoted him to assume higher responsibilities which he was capable of, but as you retire and you are no more in a position to give good bonus or promote him any further you are simply forgotten. It is often said that in a street or a market a clerk or a staff do not even look at a Director who have retired from his organization because the clerk knows that the Director now cannot give him any benefits further. These people are so selfish because they think why they should waste time, energy and money to talk or meet their bosses when they are useless for them\!  
  4536→
  4537→Above kind of incidences happen in every sphere of life, even within our relatives and “so called” friends. So why should someone be grateful. It has been proven scientifically they gratefulness leads to:
  4538→
  4539→1) Emotional well-being: Gratitude fosters positive emotions, reduces stress. Expressing appreciation leads to increased happiness and satisfaction of the soul  
  4540→2) Physical well-being: Gratitude improves physical health by promoting better sleep, reduces blood pressure and enhances immunity  
  4541→3) Mental well-being: Gratitude lowers depression and anxiety and helps in facing challenges in life  
  4542→4) Social connections: Gratitude strengthens relationships and reduces loneliness  
  4543→5) Contentment: Gratitude leads to greater contentment. It shifts focus from what one lacks to what one has promoting a sense of fulfillment
  4544→
  4545→**Practice gratefulness; you may not be aware how it improves your well-being**
  4546→
  4547→**WEIGH YOUR OPTIONS**
  4548→
  4549→I find it frustrating when one tries to figure out only the negative sides of a person, an option or an entity. Be it yourself, or a singer, or an actor, or a politician, or a team, or a political party, or a school, or whatever, there can be good things as well as bad things about it. Any human or agency or entity cannot be perfect in all parameters. But a good human or an agency or an entity has presumably many more good things than the bad things about them. You need to appreciate those good things and not ignore and focus on bad things. You can also weigh the importance of the good things and the bad things and come to a conclusion if the entity is good or bad.
  4550→
  4551→In our personal life and also in the corporate, we sometimes have many options to select from. You must weigh the positive and negative factors of each of the options and come to a conclusion on which option to choose. It is obvious that all options will have some negative points. There are people who are not happy with any option because he tries to see only the negative side of each option. These people cannot make proper and fast decisions needed in the corporate world. You have to choose an option with the greatest advantages amidst some disadvantages that it may have. 
  4552→
  4553→**SABJANTALOGY**
  4554→
  4555→This is the name of a subject which I coined after coming across some people who fit into this class of people. Just like Biology, Physiology, Oncology, Sociology, Anthropology, etc., Sabjantalogy deals with the science of knowing everything on earth.
  4556→
  4557→Some people may advise you on how to cook better, advise you on better medicines, teach you how to play a sports better, seemingly knows about any kind of industry on earth, knows about fabric and garments, gives you lecture on politics, or even on rocket science. The most astonishing part is that all these “knowhow??” has been formed on his own whims and fancies and not formed by reading books or learning from somewhere.
  4558→
  4559→Say for example, if you are an Arts graduate and have formed an idea about a medicine with your own experiment without consulting a doctor or learning it from any medical journal or reading about it from newspaper or elsewhere, is it right for you to prescribe the medicine to your friend? There can be hundreds of examples like this on various subjects.
  4560→
  4561→Today the world has lot of expertise in every field of life. If you really want to prescribe a medicine, ask the doctor, or if you really want to advise on how to make the best cakes, ten consult youtube or a chef. Why provide your “so called” knowledge which is actually wrong.
  4562→
  4563→**Don’t be a Sabtanjalogist; ask the specialist or learn about it.**  
  4564→
  4565→**DON’T BE A MISER**
  4566→
  4567→What is the purpose of earning money; to spend it wisely and reasonably; Isn’t it? But some accumulate money for their own satisfaction and not spend them for reasons not known to them. They are misers. Misers are of two types: universal misers and selfish misers. Universal misers are those who are reluctant to spend for anyone on earth. Selfish misers are those who are willing to spend only for him and family but not beyond.
  4568→
  4569→Spending must be wise and reasonable; you need to keep reasonable amount of money for medical emergencies, education and marriage of your children and for all mandatory purposes of life. Beyond that, you should spend for yourself, family and others for things that you love in life, be it for food, movies, clothes, gadget, tours and travels, hobbies or whatever that you think will be useful or make someone happy. Remember, there is no concept of Fixed Deposits in heaven. Everything that you earn in life has to be left. We have been born naked and will depart to Heaven naked. It is one life that God has given us. Enjoy it to the fullest extent. 
  4570→
  4571→How much to save, how much to invest and how much to spend may vary between individuals, but they have to be wisely and reasonably thought of practically. Allot money that you earn for savings, investment and expenses in a balanced practical manner.
  4572→
  4573→**Save & invest what is necessary. But spend too to enjoy and live your life.** 
  4574→
  4575→**SPEND TIME WITH YOUR CHILD**
  4576→
  4577→There is a joke which you will hear while your child grows up. When you are asked how tall your child has grown up, you answer by gesture with your hands apart horizontally. This is because your child leaves for school early while you are asleep and the child is asleep when you come home late from office. Therefore you have not seen him grow tall vertically on office days. Suddenly you can see him how tall he is when you are at home on Sundays. 
  4578→
  4579→However, do not take it as a simple joke. Once your child is 18 years old and pass out Class XII, he may be going out of station or abroad for higher studies and subsequently for good jobs anywhere on this earth. This means you may get to stay with your child at your home only for the first 18 years of his life and thereafter you will miss him except for a few days when the child will have time to meet you at home on his holidays.
  4580→
  4581→At the same time, you will be very busy at office trying to secure your good future year after year. But amidst this rat race, do not ignore to spend as much time as possible with your child. Remember you may not get to see him for long after he reaches 18 years of age. Your good times spent with your child will remain etched in your mind forever. Do not miss these times. It will never come back again 
  4582→
  4583→**CULTIVATE A HOBBY**
  4584→
  4585→There will be times in your life when you are very sad. Maybe your boss did not give you a promotion which you thought of sure to get, or could be your fiancé decided for a break up, or incidents like these. You would not like to be besides of your friends or family at such sad moments. You would prefer to be alone for some while. So how do you spend these times alone? Cultivate a hobby which will keep you happy and busy to tide over your sad times or after your retirement. You can learn to play cards on your own; you can learn to play an instrument; you can learn to sing; you can learn to paint; or any other hobby that will give you a sense of fulfillment.
  4586→
  4587→Music was my greatest de-stressor. Playing the harmonica and the guitar lit up my soul at sad times. These instruments are still a part of my life after retirement. 
  4588→
  4589→**DEMONSTRATE UNBIASED PROFESIONALISM**
  4590→
  4591→I have read it everywhere that even a peon would not like to wish or see your face on the street after you have retired as a Director or from a big position in the corporate. This may be true but not always. 
  4592→
  4593→As a leader, if you had shown good leadership by demonstrating correct attitude, being kind and supportive, managing people humanly with care and respect, being grateful to people who supported you, encouraging people to do well and rewarding them for good work and your actions on employees and all stakeholders always being unbiased and transparent and to top it all if you had been successful in your organization, then people, whoever knew you and know you presently hearing from others do remember and respect you even after you retire or even die. 
  4594→
  4595→Being unbiased even with your close colleagues and demonstrating your professionalism against personal bias at all times is seen as a great leadership. Such successful leaders are never forgotten.  
  4596→
  4597→**ENJOY LIFE**
  4598→
  4599→The Almighty have given you ONE life only. Even if your super rich, you have one life only. So always enjoy your life.
  4600→
  4601→How many hours do you have in a year? 24 hours a day x365 days a year \= 8760 hours. Assume you sleep 8 hours a day; therefore you spend 8 x 365 hours \= 2920 hours. Assume you work 10 hours a day (except Sundays and 12 annual holidays) and commute 2 hours a day to office; therefore you spend 12 x (365-52-12) hours \= 3612 hours. So you have (8760-2920-3612) hours, ie. 2228 hours a year excluding time for work and sleep. 2228 hours out of 8760 hours means around 25% of your life; could be less if you work more. Have you ever imagined that you have only 25% of your life free of work and sleep which you can utilize for yourself, family and friends and relatives? If you consider 5% time gone for maintaining your house, household works, taking your child to school and tuitions, purchase of daily groceries, etc. then you are left with 20% of your life time only. Sounds pretty less\! But that is the fact of life. So enjoy your life well with this 20% of your time. It shall also rejuvenate you to carry on with your work at office. You can do so by allotting time for:
  4602→
  4603→1) Savouring good food at home and restaurants  
  4604→2) Entertainment like hobbies, movies, fairs, gossiping with friends, etc  
  4605→3) Tours and Travels – local, out of station and international destinations
  4606→
  4607→Sorry, if I have missed anything that you would like to enjoy life it. Whatever it is, remember what Mahatma Gandhi said \- “Live as if you were to die tomorrow. Learn as if you were to live forever”
  4608→
  4609→**SEEING THROUGH DISRUPTORS**
  4610→
  4611→Today you will come across many disrupters during your personal and corporate life. 
  4612→
  4613→**Technological disruptors** will force you to change methods and techniques in doing your work and execute your project. As a simple example, when computers came in, typists were forced to learn software like Word, Excel, etc. to type on computers since the type writers were abolished. Any typist who did not do so lost his job.
  4614→
  4615→**Geo-political disruptors** will force you to change the way you do business. As an example the tariff episodes between USA and many other countries including India is forcing many countries to look into other avenues for their business. Such disruptors play havoc on the stock markets with their volatility.
  4616→
  4617→**Social disruptors** like break up of marriage, divorce, suicide, crimes, etc. will disturb your peace of mind which can affect your personal and corporate life. 
  4618→
  4619→**Environmental disruptors** like pollution, food adulteration, climate change, etc. is taking a toll in our health.
  4620→
  4621→In the spate of all these disruptors, you have to see through them with calm and pass them off because most are external inflicted where you have no control. It is easier said than done, but better if it can be done. You need to build a strong mind which is not easy to be disrupted. You must develop capabilities to control your mind and not allow your mind to take control of you.  
  4622→
  4623→**QUIT IF UNHAPPY**
  4624→
  4625→You can work in your company in two ways; one is with stress or pressure that is on you to perform and the other is with passion. 
  4626→
  4627→When you work with passion, you forget how long you have been working on because you work with interest not by any external force or pressure to perform. When you work for long with stress and pressure, you will undergo hormonal changes in your body harming your health. Try to find a new suitable job where you can work happily before your health deteriorates. I know finding a new job is not easy, but go on trying to save your health. Quit.  
  4628→
  4629→**CONTENTMENT IN LIFE**
  4630→
  4631→Once a middle aged officer approached me in my cabin and informed that he would like to resign because of his low pay. I was astonished to hear about the low pay and called him along with the HR manager later on. On scrutiny of his pay slip, I found that his take home pay was very less but also observed that there were lot of deductions from his salary. On deeper scrutiny I found that there were several EMIs being deducted from his salary, one for new 3BHK property, one for SUV car, one for a racing bike and another for a 75 inch LED TV. I had to only convey that the company is providing you a reasonable salary but it is due to your heavy purchases that your take home salary is meager. 
  4632→
  4633→Today many youngsters wants to have a property, car and a bike within latest 30 years of age, forget about other costly gadgets which they term them as necessary. There is no end to a materialistic world today. You must put a full stop to buying at some point of time. If you have a hatchback, you will want to have a sedan, then a SUV, then a Mercedes or a BMW, then a Ferrari, where is the end? There is no contentment in your life them, and therefore you will always remain unhappy. You need to balance your pocket. As I said earlier, you have to allot money wisely and reasonably for your savings, investment and your expenses, the total being what comes to your pocket.
  4634→
  4635→If you develop the Attitude to use money judicially and be happy with whatever you have, you will achieve a state of contentment. Contentment is about being satisfied and happy with whatever you have in life. Contentment does not look at what you do not have in life. **Achieve a state of contentment; you will be happy in life**
  4636→
  4637→**PART 30: SOME LESSONS I LEARNT**
  4638→
  4639→**The Tongue**  
  4640→What is the most important organ of human life? Obviously the heart which pumps blood more than 100,000 times a day, which means if you live for 75 years, it will pump approx. 2.75 billion times. Most miraculously, it pumps on its own; you don’t do anything for it to pump, except for taking care of it sometimes with good food and aerobic exercise. With the blood being pumped by the heart, all other organs play their vital role in their own domains.
  4641→
  4642→But what is the most important organ for living a good life? I say “the tongue”. Tongue is the only organ for the sense of taste and unhealthy but tasty food destroys our health. Food is a vital factor of our health other than some other lifestyle habits. So our tongue is responsible for our physical health. 
  4643→
  4644→Also we can speak because of our tongue. The way we speak determines our character and this character determines your relationship, be it with your family or friends or relatives or neighbours or colleagues in the corporate. The way politicians speak also determines his relationship with the public because politicians are public figures. So tongue is the main organ for your relationships. It is said that deaf and dumb do not have enemies; yes, they may have the tongue but cannot speak. 
  4645→
  4646→**Lesson**: The tongue is responsible for two major aspects in one’s life; your physical health and your relationships. Watch your tongue from now on.  
  4647→\--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 
  4648→
  4649→**True Friends**

4700→**Lesson**: Appreciate performers; Encourage and Reward them  
  4701→\------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  4702→
  4703→**Silence**  
  4704→Sometimes when we argue and protest for something, the other person may be adamant, not willing to listen to your logic, and keep on arguing to prove that you are wrong. It happens in the corporate too. Particularly when you argue with your seniors like a senior relative or your boss to put your points in place and the other person does not agree and keep on arguing, you find yourself frustrated wasting unnecessary time. In such times, I have stayed away from such people with no contacts at all for long durations. In the corporate, you can maintain silence to the extent possible except for when you need to discuss or interact. Often I have observed that your silence speaks much louder than words and helps the other person to realize that he was adamant and wrong. I have adopted this policy even with my relatives whenever required.
  4705→
  4706→**Lesson**: Silence is often the best form of protest  
  4707→\------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  4708→
  4709→**Be Respectful**  
  4710→Once my mother rebuked me for no wrong I had done. I gave her back. I did not understand the pain I inflicted on her. I loved her very much and I repented that I was harsh and aggressive. I could have replied in a much respectful manner and not insult her, however, sticking to my point of view.
  4711→
  4712→In a similar manner, I had scolded a colleague very harshly who was much junior in position to me but much senior to me by age. I saw him almost on the verge of collapsing. I learnt one big lesson in life. Even if you have to reprimand someone, particularly higher in age, for something, you can do it in a respectful manner without being harsh. If required, both can share their point of view for better understanding. Even strict punishments can be given as and when necessary, but it has to be in a respectful manner and humanly because older people probably cannot digest insult as easily as they could when they were young. Be polite and respectful to senior people.  That is a sign of good leadership.
  4713→
  4714→**Lesson**: Be respectful; It is a sign of good leadership  
  4715→\------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  4716→
  4717→**Whatever happens, it happens for good**  
  4718→Look at so many incidences I narrated before. I met with an accident while coming home from a seminar, but the incident revealed who my true friends were. I was hospitalized after a severe chest pain and underwent angioplasty, but the event taught me to live healthier and realize that Health is Wealth. I did not get promotion for long eight years in my early part of my career, but it helped me to be more perseverant and live with Trust, Hope, Faith and Confidence. When great leaders like the former Tata group Chairman and my MD facilitated me for my success, I learnt what appreciation by humble and caring leaders mean. When seniors accused me wrongly, I learnt how silence can be the best form of protest.
  4719→
  4720→Even when my father and mother died, I thought that it is for their good that their souls would be relieved from the sufferings and pain they were in. It pained me a lot on their demise, but I believed that the truth of death being certain is a creation of the Almighty for something good. Maybe that helped me to become independent from the shelter of my parents and be able to do tough work for which I was dependent on my parents. Loss of your loved one probably provides you greater strength to live on with your life, though at times the emotions of the loss is immeasurable. 
  4721→
  4722→In every undesired event, I learnt something new. Maybe that is the positive way to look at undesirable events. I am not here to prove that every undesirable event will lead to learning something new. But believing in “whatever happens, it happens for good” provides strength to live on with hope of something good lies ahead, triggers new thought process and new learning and helps to tide over such bad or sad uneventful times.

4700→**Lesson**: Appreciate performers; Encourage and Reward them  
  4701→\------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

\------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Appreciate Performers**  
Once I started doing well as the Marketing Head winning sizeable number of bids, my MD wished that the top 10 performers of the company, which included me too, be facilitated by the then Chairman of the Tata Group, Mr Cyrus Mistry. It was a moment in my corporate life to be cherished forever. Mr Mistry came to our HO at Hyderabad and facilitated me and the others with a sincere word of appreciation. I cannot forget ever the gesture of my MD and the Chairman to have done this for me. I remain ever grateful to them. And later in life, I cried when I heard the news of Mr Mistry’s death.

In another instance, when I was already succeeding as Marketing Head winning bids, my MD came to our office for a meeting with my bosses. The bosses accompanied the MD upstairs to our office and headed for his cabin past my own. When he saw me, he first came inside my cabin along with my bosses and almost fell on my feet.  I was shocked. I said “No No sir, please do not do that”. He said “Abhik, winning bids only allow our company to execute projects and everyone has a job to do in this company only when you win bids, so I am grateful to you and your team”. Tears rolled down my years on seeing his appreciation. I had no words to say.

**Lesson**: Appreciate performers; Encourage and Reward them  
\------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Silence**  
Sometimes when we argue and protest for something, the other person may be adamant, not willing to listen to your logic, and keep on arguing to prove that you are wrong. It happens in the corporate too. Particularly when you argue with your seniors like a senior relative or your boss to put your points in place and the other person does not agree and keep on arguing, you find yourself frustrated wasting unnecessary time. In such times, I have stayed away from such people with no contacts at all for long durations. In the corporate, you can maintain silence to the extent possible except for when you need to discuss or interact. Often I have observed that your silence speaks much louder than words and helps the other person to realize that he was adamant and wrong. I have adopted this policy even with my relatives whenever required.

**Lesson**: Silence is often the best form of protest  
\------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Be Respectful**  
Once my mother rebuked me for no wrong I had done. I gave her back. I did not understand the pain I inflicted on her. I loved her very much and I repented that I was harsh and aggressive. I could have replied in a much respectful manner and not insult her, however, sticking to my point of view.

In a similar manner, I had scolded a colleague very harshly who was much junior in position to me but much senior to me by age. I saw him almost on the verge of collapsing. I learnt one big lesson in life. Even if you have to reprimand someone, particularly higher in age, for something, you can do it in a respectful manner without being harsh. If required, both can share their point of view for better understanding. Even strict punishments can be given as and when necessary, but it has to be in a respectful manner and humanly because older people probably cannot digest insult as easily as they could when they were young. Be polite and respectful to senior people.  That is a sign of good leadership.

**Lesson**: Be respectful; It is a sign of good leadership  
\------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Whatever happens, it happens for good**  
Look at so many incidences I narrated before. I met with an accident while coming home from a seminar, but the incident revealed who my true friends were. I was hospitalized after a severe chest pain and underwent angioplasty, but the event taught me to live healthier and realize that Health is Wealth. I did not get promotion for long eight years in my early part of my career, but it helped me to be more perseverant and live with Trust, Hope, Faith and Confidence. When great leaders like the former Tata group Chairman and my MD facilitated me for my success, I learnt what appreciation by humble and caring leaders mean. When seniors accused me wrongly, I learnt how silence can be the best form of protest.

Even when my father and mother died, I thought that it is for their good that their souls would be relieved from the sufferings and pain they were in. It pained me a lot on their demise, but I believed that the truth of death being certain is a creation of the Almighty for something good. Maybe that helped me to become independent from the shelter of my parents and be able to do tough work for which I was dependent on my parents. Loss of your loved one probably provides you greater strength to live on with your life, though at times the emotions of the loss is immeasurable. 

In every undesired event, I learnt something new. Maybe that is the positive way to look at undesirable events. I am not here to prove that every undesirable event will lead to learning something new. But believing in “whatever happens, it happens for good” provides strength to live on with hope of something good lies ahead, triggers new thought process and new learning and helps to tide over such bad or sad uneventful times.

input.prompt:
I need to design an implementation plan for a large book manuscript editing project. Here's the context:

## The Manuscript
- File: `/Users/sagarpal/projects/baba_book/original_draft.md` (4,721 lines, ~649KB)
- Book: "Avoid Misgovernance in Corporates" by Abhik Pal
- Structure: 5 sections, 30 parts, ~108 chapters
- Chapter delimiter pattern: `**CHAPTER : [Title]**`
- Parts delimiter: `**PART X: [Title]**`
- Sections delimiter: `**SECTION X – [Title]**`
- Frontmatter (lines 1-424): Title, TOC, detailed TOC, Introduction, Preface, About Author, Acknowledgement, Disclaimer
- Main content: Lines 425-4722

## The Task
1. **Split** the original_draft.md into individual markdown files per chapter (organized by section/part)
2. **Git commit** at that split state
3. **Proofread and edit** each chapter (spelling, grammar, phrasing, sentence construction) — the user wants expert-level publishing house editorial quality
4. **Create a summary document** listing all edits/changes with references to the original text
5. **Create a Python script** to convert all markdown files to properly formatted PDFs
6. **Maximize parallelism** using sub-agents

## Design Questions to Address

1. **File organization**: How should we organize the split files? Options:
   - Flat: `chapters/01_preface.md`, `chapters/02_about_author.md`, etc.
   - Hierarchical: `sections/01_leadership/part_01/chapter_01_too_many_cxo.md`
   - By part: `parts/part_01_leadership/chapter_01.md`

2. **Splitting granularity**: Should each "chapter" be its own file, or should we split at the "part" level (30 files vs 108+ files)? Parts include intro content and "good practices" sections that aren't chapters. Consider:
   - Each Part has an intro section, multiple chapters, and a "good practices" closing
   - The frontmatter (Preface, About Author, etc.) should be separate files
   - Parts 27-30 have non-traditional "topics" rather than chapters

3. **Proofreading parallelism**: With ~108 chapters, we need an efficient strategy. Options:
   - Batch by part (30 batches)
   - Batch by section (5 batches)
   - Individual agents per chapter (too many)

4. **Summary document format**: How to reference original locations (line numbers in original_draft.md)

5. **Python PDF script**: What library to use (markdown2+weasyprint, pandoc, reportlab, etc.)

Please design a detailed, practical implementation plan that:
- Organizes files sensibly (not too deep, not too flat)
- Splits at a practical granularity
- Maximizes parallelism while keeping agent count manageable
- Specifies the exact directory structure
- Specifies the Python PDF approach
- Considers that this is a book about corporate governance in India, written by a senior executive — the editorial tone should be professional and authoritative

   345→**INTRODUCTION TO THE BOOK**
   346→
   347→Thank you for showing interest to read this book. With all honesty I say that I am not a born or a known author. I am a retired industry leader with 36 years of experience in various corporates. I wanted to share my views on Corporate Misgovernance, the title of this book, particularly with management students and young professionals who aspire to be the leaders of future so that they can understand various avoidable decisions taken by leaders in the past and enable them to refrain from committing similar mistakes when they assume top positions in the corporate like Senior Managers, Departmental Heads, Business Unit (BU) & Strategic Business Unit (SBU) Heads, Deputy General Managers (DGM) , General Managers (GM), Assistant Vice Presidents (AVP), Vice Presidents (VP) or the Chief Executive Officers (CXOs) , Presidents & Directors.
   348→
   349→The chapters of the book discusses on several practical issues as I have seen, heard and understood. I hope you will enjoy reading the book.

345→**INTRODUCTION TO THE BOOK**
   346→
   347→Thank you for showing interest to read this book. With all honesty I say that I am not a born or a known author. I am a retired industry leader with 36 years of experience in various corporates. I wanted to share my views on Corporate Misgovernance, the title of this book, particularly with management students and young professionals who aspire to be the leaders of future so that they can understand various avoidable decisions taken by leaders in the past and enable them to refrain from committing similar mistakes when they assume top positions in the corporate like Senior Managers, Departmental Heads, Business Unit (BU) & Strategic Business Unit (SBU) Heads, Deputy General Managers (DGM) , General Managers (GM), Assistant Vice Presidents (AVP), Vice Presidents (VP) or the Chief Executive Officers (CXOs) , Presidents & Directors.
   348→
   349→The chapters of the book discusses on several practical issues as I have seen, heard and understood. I hope you will enjoy reading the book. 
   350→
   351→**PREFACE**
   352→
   353→I am aware that there are thousands of books in the market on Leadership Management focusing on what Leaders should do illustrating different case studies. Though all these case studies may not be always appropriate in the Indian business environment or in the country where your organization is based, nonetheless they present very important issues handled by leaders, some of which may have had deep bearing on the viability, sustenance and profits of the organization. Leaders must learn lessons from these case studies so as to act appropriately in similar situations in future.
   354→
   355→However, this book revolves the other way round. **It focuses on what Leaders should avoid**. It cites numerous examples of wrong decisions taken by leaders, albeit in my opinion, some of which have led to serious consequences for the organization. As I grew up the ladder and eventually assumed a top position, I have come across various issues myself as well as those which I learnt from my friends who worked in different corporates at senior leadership positions. All these issues led to the compilation of this book which serves as a practical guide for young growing professionals aspiring to become leaders of future in their respective fields of expertise.
   356→
   357→Unlike many theories of management (meant for examinations?), I have narrated issues and cited decisions taken by leaders which were probably wrong in my opinion and led to various problems in the organization. In every such issue discussed, I have also tried to find out what other solution could have been more appropriate so that these problems were arrested in the first place itself.
   358→
   359→We are all human beings. Leaders too are. **“To err is human”.** People do make mistakes in life. So do leaders in organizations. Examples cited in the book are not meant to disrespect or demine any particular leader or refer to any particular individual, but rather to opine how they could have acted in a different manner. I am grateful to those same leaders belonging to various corporates from whom I have learnt so much for the growth and success in my own professional career. But, at the same time, I have analyzed how they could have taken decisions differently in many instances to avoid problems which cropped up subsequently. Some may disagree with my opinions or the solutions mentioned, nevertheless, it will provide them some food for thought on how they would like to act under such circumstances if arising in their future.
   360→
   361→The entire book has been broadly grouped into five Sections and each Section into different Parts. Each Part deals with issues of a particular domain of operations or department. Each issue of the particular domain is discussed under different chapters constituting the Part. The role of the department is first explained in the Part and thereafter each chapter against this domain discusses on a particular issue and the problems aroused and the likely solution. Some issues discussed may seem simple (not to all) and some chapters are small, may be a one pager, but I felt that such “simple or one pager” issues are worth discussing because they are important to an organization. Remember what Shakespeare said:  **“All small incidents in life together create a huge impact on our minds”.** Similarly many small decisions in the corporate may create huge impact on the organization over time. And hence the importance of recognizing issues, big or small. Some of you may already be aware of some of the issues cited and they may feel that these issues are a commonplace not worth mentioning. However, I have sometimes observed that leaders still take such inappropriate decisions and therefore these issues deserve mention and discussion for the benefit of all.
   362→
   363→Almost all the chapters of the book are in reference to construction projects in the Indian context since construction projects encompasses different domains of management. All the chapters are purposefully hilariously titled to draw your attention on the core issue. 
   364→
   365→At the end of each Part, I have also listed some good practices which may be adopted by leaders to create a **“feel good factor”,** a proper corporate governance model and a good working environment amongst employees in the organization. It’s up to you to adopt should you feel it as relevant. 
   366→
   367→In Part 26 of this book, I have explored some new concepts which come across my mind. I leave it to your wisdom to adopt them or ignore. Annexures in Part 27 of the book illustrates some concepts for your better understanding. Part 28 provide some personal advices which are interrelated between your personal and corporate life. These issues are often ignored while we are busy at work in office but they affect your life, family, work, work-life balance and your finances and most importantly your health \- mental, physical and spiritual. Hence it’s importance. Part 29 narrates some lessons I learnt in the corporate which helped me to get along with people at different challenging times.
   368→
   369→As a SBU Head, I had the privilege to work with my BU Heads, Departmental Heads of Strategy, Marketing & Business Development, Advertising, Designs & Engineering, Bidding & Costing, Project Management, Site Operations, Production Management, Supply Chain Management, Fleet and Equipment Management, Stores Management, Quality, Safety, Human Resources, Administration, Contracts & Law, Finance & Accounts, Audit, Information Technology, Learning and Development, Insurance, Ethics, ESG, CSR and learnt the varied dynamics of different domains of operations. These experiences gave me a detailed insight to taking right decisions and helped me to flag different issues discussed in the book.  
   370→   
   371→Right from beginning of my career, I had an inclination to analyze different decisions taken by leaders in various corporates under various circumstances. Such observations were of great interest to me. As I grew up with more experience, I felt that many of these decisions taken could have been different and I always wondered on what could have been done right. After I retired, I thought that the only way I can convey my thoughts on right governance to young professionals and management students was to write this book. 
   372→
   373→Small notes before you start reading the book.  
   374→Use of the words like “He/his/him” also means and includes “she/her’s/her”  
   375→Reference to People in the organization means the employees  
   376→Words like Organization, Company & Corporate are interchangeable.  
   377→Words like “you” may also refer to the organization.  
   378→Words like Project and Contract are interchangeable.  
   379→Words like Order, Award and Contract are interchangeable.  
   380→Words like Bid and Tender are interchangeable.  
   381→Words like vendor, supplier, and subcontractor are interchangeable.  
   382→Words like contractor may mean the organization itself  
   383→Words like specifications, tender or bid documents are interchangeable.  
   384→Words like client and customer are interchangeable.
   385→
   386→Last but not the least, you may mail your suggestions and feedback to [abhik.pal.tata@gmail.com](mailto:abhik.pal.tata@gmail.com) so that I too can learn from your experience and incorporate them as deemed fit in the next edition of the book. I wish all of you a great leadership in future. 
   387→
   388→**Happy reading.**

   395→**ABOUT THE AUTHOR**
   396→
   397→I am a 1984 Civil Engineering graduate from IIEST, formerly known as Bengal Engineering College, Howrah, West Bengal, a premier Engineering institute of India. I am credited with an Advanced Diploma in Management from IMT, Ghaziabad which also a premier institute of management in India.
   398→
   399→In the 36 years of my career, I had a stint of 30 years in three Tata Group companies. In the later years, I was the Head of Business Development, Marketing and Bidding for 10 years in Tata Projects and subsequently assumed the position of **Executive Vice President & SBU Head** of a specific business domain in Tata Projects for 3 years before I hanged my boots.
   400→
   401→In the initial eight years of my professional life, I was a Design/Structural Engineer involved in the designs of different kind of structures like multistoried buildings, cooling towers, high voltage transmission line towers, etc. using software tools like STAAD and SAP. I was also into hardcore software development for designs of Reinforced Concrete Structures (RCC) using Fortran 77 as the programming language. After a eight year stint in the Designs domain, I was transferred to Marketing department and started my career in the field of management. 
   402→
   403→Outside the corporate world, I am an avid lover of music, particularly instrumental music, and I play the harmonica and guitar even today, which kept me going through my bad days and times of stress while in the corporate.
   404→
   405→I was fortunate to meet and talk to Sir Ratan Tata only once in my life in 2018 during an event on “Quality” of the Tata group in Mumbai and I adored his personality, grace, humbleness, wisdom and knowledge with all his magnificent achievements at the back of my mind. This was one of the best moments of my corporate life, a moment which I would cherish for the rest of my life.

She was confident that this new kind of book will inspire youngsters to learn and act better as future leaders. Her continuous inspiration over the last two years helped me to complete writing this book.

   412→

She has always been highly inquisitive to learn on how employees can develop themselves to become great leaders and while narrating many such experiences of mine to her complex questions gave me important insights to cover some issues in the book. She too scrutinised the contents of the book which helped me to reach the final stage of the book.
   414→

   416→
   417→**DISCLAIMER**
   418→
   419→I reiterate that the issues cited in the book do not refer to any particular leader and is not meant to demine or disrespect any leader. These issues as observed in different corporates including those where my friends worked as senior leaders, have been discussed only to understand the complexities and problems that wrong decisions can create and how decisions could have been taken differently, albeit again, in my opinion.
   420→
   421→The issues are important to be discussed because such issues may crop up in any organization. However, the proposed solutions to these issues which have been mentioned to have created problems in the organization may be differently opined by others; nevertheless, it provides an insight to the youngsters for evoking their thought process. That’s the objective of the book. When these youngsters would become our future leaders, their wisdom and experience will be their best judge to decide.
   422→
   423→The book is not meant for senior leaders because they must have gone through most of these issues at one point of time in their career and they have already attained maturity and wisdom to deal with them. It is meant for management students and young professionals who may be enlightened with different issues that leaders of organisations come across so that they can deal with them first hand when they become leaders in the future. 
   424→
   425→**SECTION 1 – LEADERSHIP**
   426→
   427→**PART 1**
   428→
   429→**CXO/SBU/BU/DEPARTMENT HEAD LEVEL LEADERSHIP**

1130→For example, assume that the cost of visits, marketing and setting up an office in a foreign country is Rs 3 Crores in a year. A leader, say, spends two years into marketing in the country before he starts getting a business. After two years, say, the organization gets two orders of Rs 100 Cr each, both with a completion period of 2 years. Assume that each project is completed by 50% each year and the projects make a margin of 5%. Then here is the Marketing cost-returns matrix (All figures are in Rs Cr)
  1131→
  1132→Year	Opening	Cost		Orders  	Executed 	Profit		Closing  
  1133→	Balance	incurred	Received	Revenues	Margin	Balance  
  1134→1	0		3		0		0		0		0  
  1135→2	0		3		0		0		0		0  
  1136→3	0		3		200		100		5		100  
  1137→4	100		3		200		150		7.5		150  
  1138→5	150		3		200		175		8.75		175  
  1139→6	175		3		200		187.5		9.375		187.5  
  1140→Total after 6 years:	18		800		612.5		30.625
  1141→
  1142→Above was a simple exercise. I have not considered the time value of money to make it simple to understand. You can observe that after 6 years, while you have incurred a cumulative cost of Rs 18 Cr, you have earned profits of Rs 30.625 Cr. But for this, you need a continuous flow of orders and a continuous executing to earn the profits.  
  1143→    
  1144→Future business is all about estimates from your experience. You have to be rational in your expectations considering the business potential available in the country, the competition existing, the strategy you employ to secure business, etc. If your expectations were as above, it makes sense to continue marketing efforts for the next couple of years as you can find from the Cost-Returns matrix though the gestation period in the above example was 2 years, in other words, there was no earnings in the first two years but the expenses had to be incurred.  
  1145→
  1146→**The salient points are:**
  1147→
  1148→1. Evaluate MoTs after every foreign tour to determine progress made  
  1149→2. Abandon or continue foreign tours based on progressive results and future estimated returns  
  1150→3. Determine Marketing-cost matrix to understand worthwhileness of your efforts
  1151→
  1152→**THE “FORCE YOUR STYLE” SYNDROME**
  1153→
  1154→Leaders, irrespective of whether you are the MD or a Director or any CXO or a SBU Head or a BU Head or a Departmental Head, have different styles of leadership patterns. These patterns are generally developed by individual leaders while they were growing up the ladder to senior positions and eventually became top leaders.
  1155→
  1156→All leadership styles are different from each other, some may be effective for some, and some others may be effective for others. There are nothing called right or wrong kinds of leadership. All leadership styles have advantages and disadvantages and that is why some leaders tactfully employ different leadership styles at different times.
  1157→
  1158→Once there was a CXO who always insisted his style of work to his subordinate. The CXO thought that his kind of leadership style is the only effective style that everyone needs to adopt. The CXO did not appreciate the subordinate having a different style of effective leadership and did not think that forcing upon him another kind of leadership style will not help him to achieve desired results. A top leader must be interested in achieving the result irrespective of the leadership style that his subordinate follows so long the leadership style is effective, ethical and SMART. Bulldozing a leader’s style down below is a sign of being adamant and displaying dictatorship.
  1159→
  1160→One must develop his own style of leadership and the style which his boss or his leader adopts may not necessarily be copied. The leader must also be democratic allowing his subordinate to follow his own style of leadership so long the results are ethically effective. 
  1161→
  1162→I have not discussed here the different style of leadership in practice which you can get to read in any leadership book. This chapter is on how you expect others to practice leadership. Interestingly, long time back, I had come across an article in a newspaper narrating the Gandhian style and the Hitlarian style of leadership. I was impressed by its different styles and I used to follow in the corporate having found it very effective. These styles have been described in an Annexure later.  
  1163→   
  1164→**The salient points are:**
  1165→
  1166→1. Develop your own style of leadership as you feel right. Don’t copy styles of your leaders unnecessarily  
  1167→2. You may adopt different styles to be effective in different circumstances
  1168→
  1169→**THE “DELIBERATE PROCASTINATION” SYNDROME**
  1170→
  1171→Once, one BU Head, who was reporting to his SBU Head, was given the responsibility of securing a negotiated project in Africa. The award of project required a competitive pricing as well as lot of documentation to prove the credibility and experience of the organization. 
  1172→
  1173→Meetings were held occasionally and documents had to be revised or modified in line with the client’s requirements from time to time. Sometimes there were new documents required to be submitted. The SBU Head was to retire in a few months from then. The SBU Head, during review meetings, enquired regarding the progress of the expected award and found that the progress was quite slow.
  1174→
  1175→Actually, the BU Head was processing the case slowly. He was not in a mood to speed up the award process, in which case the SBU Head would take the major credit for receiving the award before his retirement was due. The objective of the BU Head was to go slow in such a manner that the award would be received after the retirement of the SBU Head, in which case he would have the entire liberty to claim full credit for bagging the award.
  1176→
  1177→The above attitude is condemnable. Firstly, the SBU Head also had a role to play to receive the award based on his initial negotiations. The BU Head trying to discredit him by receiving the award intentionally after the SBU Head’s retirement is not right in its first place. Secondly, life teaches that opportunity does not come again and again. In case you intentionally delay to receive an award, you risk your organization by giving your competitor to sneak in and grab the opportunity in which case you may not be considered for the award. Thirdly, every organization has quarterly and annual targets to be achieved. By delaying receipt of awards, you are actually depriving the organization to achieve the quarterly or annual targets. Even if the annual targets were in place, the award received would have increased the annual orders leading to better increments and bonus to the employees.
  1178→
  1179→Therefore the SBU Head must be vigilant if any of his subordinates are intentionally delaying any process in the organization. For this, regular reviews should be in place and the problem of delay identified right at the beginning. If required, the responsibility can be shifted to some other honest individual who would try his best to speed up the process of an expected award. Deliberate procrastination must be dealt with severely.  
  1180→
  1181→**The salient points are:**
  1182→
  1183→1. Deliberate procrastination is highly condemned. It may rob the organization of opportunities  
  1184→2. Intentional procrastination to credit oneself and discredit someone else who also deserves credit is a crime. Such leaders must be dealt with sternly  
  1185→3. Regular reviews must be held to arrest deliberate procrastination. If required, the responsibility can be shifted to others  
  1186→   
  1187→
  1188→**SOME GOOD PRACTICES**
  1189→
  1190→**Skip level meetings**  
  1191→Often the level below yours do not get chance to voice their views to your bosses. In fact sometimes the reporting level tries to suppress the voices of their subordinates to their bosses for vested reasons. In a skip level meeting with a leader, the leader keeps his reporting level in abeyance and interacts with the employees’ one level below so that the leader is aware of important things that may have been ignored or kept secret by his reporting level. Understanding the business processes may be clearer during interaction in the skip level meetings. There could be good suggestions too coming up during these discussions which the reporting level might have ignored.
  1192→
  1193→**Democratic leadership**  
  1194→In major decisions, take inputs from your team members. After all it is the team members who have to deliver. By considering their inputs, you raise their morale, motivation, level of engagement and confidence. Do not be autocratic for major decisions. 
  1195→
  1196→**Supportive delegation**  
  1197→When you delegate jobs to people, make sure you provide them with the right support and guidance. Without support, your delegation will be an instruction without results. Proper support engages people well avoiding confusion and frustration.
  1198→
  1199→**Team building**  
  1200→When you build a team with members having diverse capabilities, you succeed in your ventures. Individual brilliance seldom achieves corporate goals. Developing team creates a supportive work environment.
  1201→
  1202→**Monthly MIS review**  
  1203→This will track progress in all aspects of business. It will give insights to areas of improvement. The problem areas can be identified and addressed during such meetings. The clients and vendors can be immediately informed about critical issues that the organization is facing. Different Heads can be roped in as required to boost the performance in projects. Each project should be reviewed in details including its financials and action points noted. The overheads may also be determined to keep a track of the same. Market share analysis must also be tracked every month including those of the competitors.
  1204→
  1205→**Quarterly revision of targets**  
  1206→If need be, based on unknown factors earlier and the market conditions, the KRAs may be tweaked to make them reasonable and SMART. Goals which are SMART are accepted by employees with a challenge.  
  1207→
  1208→**Monthly meetings for work environment and organizational issues**  
  1209→Apart from business results understood from MIS reviews, work related issues are identified in these meetings. There could be areas concerning software requirements, or modifying existing processes, or boosting the hardware of the existing IT systems, or restructuring ergonomics in the office space, etc. Solving work related issues leads to better job satisfaction amongst employees, optimizes time and provides a “feel good” factor, all leading to a better work culture in the organization.
  1210→
  1211→**Monthly recognition Certificates to the best performer**  
  1212→Good monthly performance should be rewarded. This will create a healthy competition amongst employees in the future which will improve organizational performance as a whole. Also the morale of the winners will be boosted for better performance. Non winners can be more enthusiastic to try harder to get an award in the next month.
  1213→
  1214→**Unconventional thinking**  
  1215→Support and appreciate unconventional thinking. Many new thoughts may not be found to be effective, but some may be exceptionally brilliant. Arouse the culture of unconventional thinking in the organisation. May be you have a quarterly session for the purpose. 
  1216→
  1217→**Find the right successor**  
  1218→Depending on potential, you have to pick someone and train him as your successor. Leaders must ensure the future of an organization and hence its importance.
  1219→
  1220→**Promote knowledge and skills to employees through Training**  
  1221→There is no end to learning in our lives. From a newly born baby till death, we learn so many things in our lives on a daily basis. Coming to corporates, similarly, training in different fields of expertise must be carried on to enhance the capabilities of employees. This will help employees improve and achieve desired goals of the organization.
  1222→
  1223→**Meeting each Department quarterly**  
  1224→Each supporting Department has issues of their own. All such issues cannot be looked into in a monthly MIS meeting which is basically driven to review business results. Yet Departmental issues play a critical role in the performance of an organization. Hence looking into issues faced by each Department, at least once in a quarter, and resolving them is of significant importance.
  1225→
  1226→**Be unbiased towards all employees**  
  1227→Treat all equally based on performance and potential, irrespective of sex, caste, creed, colour, religion and also importantly irrespective of your personal relationship. This will create a healthy environment in the organization. Employees will believe what is being said and done is right. It will create trust amongst employees and also towards the organization and foster healthy environment. If you are treated equally, there will be a tendency to accept if you are wrong at times. It builds up an atmosphere of true spirit in the organization. 
  1228→
  1229→**Communicate well**  
  1230→Once every month, send a communication across the organization on all the developments that happened in the month and the future endeavours discussed. Employees will take pride to work in the organization knowing well about the developments and likely future. Proper communication will also arrest rumours and grape vine, if any, floating in and around the organization.  
  1231→
  1232→**Wish on birthdays**  
  1233→Ask HR team to send your wishes for birthday of your employees. Birthday wishes brings happiness to employees, that too from senior leaders. This creates togetherness in an organization. Employees feel proud that their leaders remember their days of joy
  1234→
  1235→**Get Well Soon cards**  
  1236→Ask HR to send Get Well Soon cards to employees suffering from an ailment and on leave. Employees would feel that they are cared for. They will always give their best performance to their leaders and the organization once they resume work.
  1237→
  1238→**SECTION 2 – PEOPLE**
  1239→
  1240→**PART 2**
  1241→
  1242→**PEOPLE, THE EMPLOYEES**
  1243→
  1244→**The role of people in a corporate**  
  1245→As we know and say, people are the most important assets of an organisation. An organisation does not comprise of a name and a huge building made of bricks and mortar. It’s the people who make the name and fame of an organisation; it’s the people who execute the business to achieve its objectives and build reputation for the organization. 
  1246→
  1247→Other assets are also important. But unlike the yesteryears, they are comparatively easier to build. For example, an organisation today, even if it’s size is small, has multiple sources to obtain finance, be it banks or other financial institutions, at low interest rates. With such finance, it can purchase good quality equipment and other infrastructure, software and hardware to conduct its business. With money, you can easily invest good quality assets for your business.
  1248→
  1249→But what about good quality professionals? Unfortunately, they don’t come with an ISO stamp as a product or service does. Yes, they do come with academic degrees behind them, but it has been proven time and again that the best academic professionals of reputed universities or colleges are not necessarily the best experienced and talented people to deliver in organisations. Academic degrees do provide the necessary criteria to appear for an interview and are indicative of supposedly good performance, but the HR Head has to be very cautious to select a professional who can practically deliver the best in the industry.

3880→The top management should provide adequate manpower and finances to implement CSR activities successfully.
  3881→
  3882→**Visit CSR sites annually**: This is normally not done. If you have created a permanent facility as a part of CSR, visit them one by one to see if they are maintained and are being used most effectively for the purpose of them being created. This will give the organization satisfaction as to have created a permanent facility for the deprived.
  3883→
  3884→**Provide funds to CSR facilities for maintenance**: As discussed earlier, a facility created for a good purpose needs to be maintained periodically so that the facility can be used permanently by the deprived. Therefore, all CSR facilities must have a dedicated fund so that the maintenance of the facility can be undertaken from time to time.
  3885→
  3886→**Donate :** As a part of CSR, you and the organisation can donate to various NGOs so that they can the serve the poor in several ways.  
  3887→
  3888→**SECTION 5 – ARTICLES**
  3889→
  3890→**PART 27**
  3891→
  3892→**NEW CONCEPTS**
  3893→
  3894→Innovation is the mother of invention. When Edison’s trial for lighting the bulb failed a hundred of times, people laughed. When Einstein discovered the gravitational theory, people laughed. People often laugh at new things thinking they are a joke. But eventually all such innovations become reality. 
  3895→
  3896→Therefore, try to innovate and find new ways to do things which are smarter, easier and shorter. Sometimes when you adopt new practices, people may not like to adopt or some may not like them because of sheer jealousy, but eventually people will adopt when they find them better in due course of time. You have to give time and explain about its benefits compared to earlier practices. There is a very good saying in English – “the only constant today is change”. So, change, change and change but change for the better so that employees would welcome and adopt such changes.
  3897→
  3898→I have picked up certain issues in the following chapters which can be subjected to introspection by you. You may like some, and not like some. But they give you some food for thought.
  3899→
  3900→**CHAPTER : PROJECT HEALTH CARD**
  3901→
  3902→Have you seen a job card maintained by the car maintenance engineer when you take a car to a workshop? Have you seen your own health card comprising results of your blood tests and other tests performed by the diagnostic laboratory?
  3903→
  3904→Similarly why not a Project Health card? Many would say what is new about this? MIS on Projects display various project data for managers to take necessary action. That’s 100% true. However, I would like to introduce some new parameters which I have not seen any MIS, yet I feel they are important indications to the health of a project. Let us discus one by one.
  3905→
  3906→**Time lapsed**   
  3907→Suppose a high voltage transmission line project involves casting of 500 foundations, erecting of 500 tower structures and stringing (ie. Installing of overhead wires on top of the erected towers) of 499 spans between towers. Depending on physical time required, I put a weightage of 40% on foundations, 35% on tower erection and balance 25% on stringing.
  3908→
  3909→At any point of time, suppose 200 foundations are cast, 150 towers erected and 100 spans strung. Let us calculate the time lapsed.
  3910→
  3911→Time lapsed \= (0.4 \* 200/500) \+ (0.35 \* 150/500) \+ (0.25 \* 100/499)  
  3912→		\= 0.16 \+ 0.105 \+ 0.126  
  3913→		\= 0.391, ie. 39.10% of time taken 
  3914→
  3915→Suppose the completion period for the above project was 24 months.  
  3916→Therefore time lapsed \= 0.391 \* 24 \=  9.384 months, say 9 months for simplicity
  3917→
  3918→Now, if the actual time spent from the date of award is 12 months to complete those 200 foundations, 150 towers. 100 spans of stringing, then your project is delayed by 3 months (12-9) because you have actually done a 9 month progress in a physical 12 month spent. But if the actual time spent from the date of award is 7 months, then your project is 2 months (9-2) ahead of schedule.
  3919→
  3920→The above example is a simple exercise with 3 activities only. You may list down more number of major activities (major meaning taking significant time) and do the exercise from time to time. This is a great way to determine the progress of a project broadly.
  3921→
  3922→**Money earned**  
  3923→Every activity is associated with billing. Say foundations comprise of 25% of total price, tower erections 45% of total price and stringing balance 30% on total price.
  3924→
  3925→Taking the same example as above,  
  3926→Money earned \= (0.25 \* 200/500) \+ (0.45 \* 150/500) \+ (0.30 \* 100/499)  
  3927→     \= 0.10 \+ 0.135 \+ 0.06  
  3928→                            \= 0.295, ie. 29.5% of total price 
  3929→
  3930→Therefore in 39.10% of time consumed, I have earned 29.50% of total contract price. In other words, I have spent more percentage of time than I have earned as a percentage of total contract price.
  3931→
  3932→It is obvious that the above two percentages cannot be same, however they should not differ much because the hardships and time consumed in executing an activity should be correlated to the price quoted for the activity. If the money earned % is far less than the time lapsed %, it means that the activities have not been quoted appropriately.
  3933→
  3934→Also intelligent bidders tend to front load the activities, which mean quoting more prices for activities to be done in the beginning and quoting lesser for activities to be done later. In this manner, you can better the money earned % with respect to time lapsed % meaning you earn more with respect to time. This leads the project to a better cash flow. However, there is a limit to which front loadings can be done otherwise the unit prices of the activities may seem to be unreasonable.
  3935→
  3936→**CHAPTER : THE PRODUCTIVITY MEASUREMENT**
  3937→
  3938→We know Employee Turnover is the turnover, that is, revenues generated per employee. Here there is a catch. Should the number of employees include contractual employees. My answer is yes. If we understand the principle, contractual employees appointed in the name of the organisation, should be considered since they are wholly contributing to the turnover of the company. However, outsourced employees employed through third parties may not be considered but their CTC should be considered in calculating the employee overheads. Let me give you an example to illustrate interesting facts.
  3939→
  3940→Assume,  
  3941→**SBU 1**  
  3942→Turnover \= Rs 2400 Cr  
  3943→Number of employees \= 1500  
  3944→CTC of above employees \= Rs 141 Cr
  3945→
  3946→**SBU 2**  
  3947→Turnover \= Rs 3400 Cr  
  3948→Number of permanent employees \= 3000  
  3949→CTC of above employees \= Rs 162 Cr
  3950→
  3951→**Calculation of companys’s employee turnover and overheads**  
  3952→Total turnover \= 2400 \+ 3400 \= Rs 5800 Cr  
  3953→Total employees \= 1500 \+ 3000 \= 4500  
  3954→Employee turnover \= 5800/4500 \= Rs 1.29 Cr  
  3955→Total CTC \= 141 \+ 162 \= Rs 303 Cr  
  3956→Employee overheads \= 303/5800 \= 5.22%
  3957→
  3958→**Calculation of SBU wise employee turnover and overheads**  
  3959→**SBU 1**  
  3960→Employee turnover \= 2400/1500 \= Rs 1.60 Cr (better performed than SBU 2\)  
  3961→Employee overheads \= Rs 141/2400 \= 5.875%  
  3962→**SBU 2**  
  3963→Employee turnover \= 3400/3000 \= Rs 1.13 Cr  
  3964→Employee overheads \= Rs 162/3400 \= 4.76% (lower overheads than SBU 1\)
  3965→
  3966→**The concept of Employee Productivity Cost**  
  3967→See the fallacy above. SBU 1 performed better in terms of productivity but had  
  3968→higher overheads which is not desirable. Higher overheads may still lead to  
  3969→better productivity, that is not the problem. The problem is confusion arises because of the number game, that is, the number of employees.
  3970→
  3971→Actually, the number should not matter. What matters is the total cost of employees and the turnover generated. A SBU may have a higher number of employees with lower salaries (which tends lower the Employee Turnover) as compared to a SBU having a lesser number of employees with higher salaries.  
  3972→   
  3973→So, let me introduce a different concept of Employee Productivity Cost, that is Turnover in lakhs generated per One lakh employee cost. This ignores the number of employees in the calculations.
  3974→
  3975→In the above example,  
  3976→Employee Productivity Cost \= 5800 x 100/(303 x 100\) \= Rs 19.14 Lakhs  
  3977→Employee Productivity Cost of SBU 1 \= 2400 x 100/(141 x 100\) \= Rs 17.02 Lakhs  
  3978→Employee Productivity Cost of SBU 2 \= 3400 x 100/(162 x 100\) \= Rs 20.99 Lakhs
  3979→
  3980→What were the Employee Turnover calculated before?  
  3981→Employee Turnover of SBU 1 was Rs 1.60 Cr and,  
  3982→Employee Turnover of SBU 2 was Rs 1.13 Cr
  3983→
  3984→So Management would tend to believe that SBU 1 have done better than SBU 2 because their employee turnover is higher. But actually SBU 2 has done better because One lakh of employee cost of SBU 2 generated a turnover of Rs 20.99 Lakhs as compared to SBU 1 generating Rs 17.02 Lakhs. And since money is all that matters in a company so far the financials are concerned, the concept of Employee Turnover Cost is a better concept than the Employee Turnover.
  3985→
  3986→I am not writing off the concept of Employee Turnover. It does give a sense of the productivity in terms of turnover produced per employee but what I am trying to determine is that the Employee Productivity Cost is a better measure of employee productivity.
  3987→
  3988→To conclude, what figures of Employee Turnover, Employee Overheads and Employee Productivity Cost are good benchmarks? Well, that depends on the industry tou are in. You may calculate your figures vis-a-vis that of your competitors and arrive at benchmarks.
  3989→
  3990→**CHAPTER : NON FINANCE FOR FINANCE**
  3991→
