**PART 29: PERSONAL ADVICE**

I feel that those who consider these advices will go a long way in their corporate lives. These advices are entirely personal and therefore I leave it to your wisdom whether to consider or not.

**ATTITUDE**

Assume A is designated as 1, B as 2, C as 3 and so on till Z as 26\.  
Now add the numbers against each character of ATTITUDE, ie. 1(A)+20(T)+20(T)+9(I)+20(T)+21(U)+4(D)+5(E) \= 100

Probably ATTITUDE is the only English word whose designated numeric numbers add to 100\. This is just a coincidence, however the number 100 attached to the word ATTITUDE signifies immense importance.

You will observe that some people may have the right vision, knowledge and skills but lacks attitude to accomplish a certain work, be it for any reason. The right attitude steers your knowledge and skills into action thereby accomplishing the work in hand. If you do not have the right attitude, you cannot succeed in life however knowledgeable you may be and however great skills you may possess. 

**Have the correct attitude always; it will pay rich dividends in your life.**

**BE HUMANE**

When a child grows up, relatives and neighbours often ask what you would like to be when you grow up. Some say doctor, some say engineer, and some even say pilot. But I taught my child, which I suppose every parent should, that when asked about his future, he should say that he wants to be a good human being apart from anything else that someone wants to be. Being a good human being goes a long way in your life, even in the corporate world as you may have heard about Sir Ratan Tata. Good human beings can manage and deal with people better in the corporate. This is an advantage to them apart from the vision, knowledge and skills they possess. 

**PEOPLE MANAGEMENT**

I still remember one of the wise words that my boss taught when I stepped into the Marketing domain in my earlier career. “Listen Abhik, while you build your career in Marketing, you will come across various kind of people; good and bad, honest and dishonest, polite and angry, knowledgeable and not knowledgeable, helpful and not so helpful, co-operative and jealous, kind and unkind, biased and unbiased, etc., so you must (1) learn how to deal with all these kind of people because you cannot simply ignore their presence in the organization and (2) learn how to say “No’ with a smile because you cannot say yes to proposals and statements that you feel are not right.

In fact when I assumed the position of Marketing Head and stayed on for ten years and eventually became the Business Head and stayed on for the last three years of my career, I found that managing people was the most difficult job for me. As an organization, I encountered many technical and financial challenges but I had the support of expertise in all the fields of operation, be it in Designs, Projects, Supply Chain, Quality, Safety or Finance or any other department whatsoever to overcome these challenges. But Heads and senior people often had different opinions, sometimes judgmental, contradictory and sometimes steered by vested interest, jealousy and anger. So hearing out everyone, using my vision and knowledge and taking a final decision was often very challenging because the final decision did not adhere to some of the opinions yet I had to convince all as to why the final decision has been taken.   
Every human being has an ego which they wish to be satisfied. You cannot ignore people who work with you, yet you need to take decisions which are not in line with some of the opinions. 

People are very sensitive. All their opinions must be heard to. At times, their opinions may be disregarded and at times theirs could be the best option. You have to treat all your colleagues humanly with due respect. And you must have the right attitude and power to convince all the reasons for your final decision.  
   
Hence I found man management the most difficult job in my career as well as the most interesting and challenging. 

**Develop man management skills to succeed in life.**

**FIRST OPPORTUNITY**

Opportunity comes and goes. Sometimes it comes only once. You should grab opportunity at the first time itself, provided it’s reasonably good, lest a better one does not come later. You have to hold your greed and grab a reasonable opportunity at the first instance itself because most of the times a better opportunity does not come again.

Once in my early part of my career, I was selected for a management course at the Imperial Institute of Management in UK by the company. I was reluctant because I was comfortable in my job at Kolkata and I thought to undergo the course after a year or two and till then to earn some money. I due course of time, the sponsorship was stopped by the company and that opportunity never came again. Eventually, much later on, I had to settle for a Advanced Diploma Course in Management from the Institute of Management Technology (IIMT) through a distance learning mode. 

Once in a Maruti True Value showroom, when I did not have enough money to buy a new car, I spotted a car which I wished to buy since the price was very reasonable. I kept it on my buy list. In greed to get a better deal, I visited another showroom but could not get a deal as good as the earlier one. I rushed to the first showroom again to seal the deal but the car was sold by then. Eventually I purchased a car but at a higher price.

Most of the times I have observed that the first opportunity I got were the best opportunity. And in case the second opportunity was better, leave the first one and take the second if possible and if not possible then don’t repent.

**Grab the first opportunity rather than be greedy to grab a better one later**.

**INSURANCE**

Insurance is a cover for an individual to safeguard his interest. For example life insurance will safeguard against the livelihood costs of the nominee and his family in case of death, health insurance will safeguard against the medical costs for treatment, property insurance will safeguard against EMI payments by the nominee in case of death, house insurance will safeguard against the costs for damage of house due to calamities, cyber insurance will safeguard against cyber frauds and threats.

You have to pay premiums for taking such insurances. But many wise people argue foolishly at the end of a year that they have been profitable by not taking insurance cover and thus paying premiums since nothing happened to them in the year. Financially it may be so but technically not. No one desires that something bad happens to them ever; however, one should think that you need to de-risk such undesirable events. Hence is the importance of insurance. All individuals are advised to take insurances. 

**Insurance is a cover for risk; the premium is for attaining peace of mind.**  
       
**HEALTH**

Health is wealth. We understand it better as we age and we sometimes find ourselves in unhealthy conditions or become sick. While we are young, being sick is rare and so we then think Money is Wealth, not Health. However, today many youngsters are prone to sickness due to many factors, personal and environmental.

As we remain busy in our corporate life, health, the most precious asset that one possess, is often ignored. Continuous such ignorance may lead to health complications later on. One must sincerely take care of his health while on service. Do not postpone it till retirement. Maintain health right from day one.

Health pertains to different domains of an individual;  
Physical health is about taking care of your body;

4030→**PART 28 : ANNEXURES**
  4031→
  4032→Annexures deals with subjects which are sometimes wrongly interpreted or less understood. Therefore I thought to discuss on some subjects in this Part to provide clarity to young professionals. However, you can delve on these subjects in various documents for a deeper understanding.
  4033→
  4034→**ANNEXURE: VISION & MISSION**
  4035→
  4036→Many employees are not clear what a Mission or a Vision of an organization is. Let us try to understand.
  4037→
  4038→Every organisation has a Mission and a Vision and they emphasize this to its employees and the outside world through a statement called the Vision and Mission statement.
  4039→
  4040→A mission statement defines the organization's business, its objectives, and how it will reach these objectives. A vision statement details where the organization aspires to go.
  4041→
  4042→Sometimes the terms “mission statement” and “vision statement” are used interchangeably or even combined into a single statement.  
  4043→But they mean two very different things. Your mission statement is what your company is doing right now, while your vision statement is what you hope to achieve in the future – where you are in this moment versus where you’re going.
  4044→
  4045→Let me give a practical example. If someone asks you, “So, what do you do currently?” you might say, “I’m a Bidding Manager in a large-sized construction company”. But, what if they asked you, “What do you want to be in five or 10 years from now?” Your answer might be a bit different, right? Maybe you’d say, “My goal is to become the Strategic Business Head and ultimately would like to be the Managing Director of the organisation”
  4046→
  4047→In the above example, as a Bidding Manager, your Mission is to win many bids and have the largest market share but your Vision may be to head the business and eventually become the MD of the organisation.
  4048→
  4049→**ANNEXURE: DPR TO CONTRACTS CLOSURE**
  4050→
  4051→There are various stages of a project executed by a customer through its selected contractor. Let us understand the entire process step by step.
  4052→
  4053→1\. **DPR** \- Every customer studies in details the benefits of a project when implemented. It studies the requirements to build the project, ie. Land, cost of acquisition, permissions from different authorities, finance, manpower and equipment infrastructure and the timelines of construction. A Detail Project Report (DPR) is prepared comprising of above details which is subsequently approved by concerned authority.
  4054→
  4055→2\. **NIT** \- The customer publishes in brief, in newspapers, websites, etc., about the requirement of the project and invites prospective contractors to participate in the bidding for the project. This is called the Notice Inviting Tender (NIT) which also mentions the last date of procuring the bidding documents.
  4056→
  4057→3\. **Bid** \- Once the NIT is published, interested contractors purchase the bidding documents and prepare the bid. The last date of submission of the bid is also mentioned in the bidding documents. The bid generally has two parts, one is the Techno-commercial bid, which comprises of all technical and commercial documents required to be submitted in the bid, and the other is the Price or Financial bid which comprises of all Price Schedules detailing item wise and total prices with a Summary of Price Schedules. The compilation of all these documents is sealed in packets and submitted to the customer within the due date of submission is called the Bid. A bid needs to be valid for a period of time, could be three to six months, as prescribed. This means the bidder cannot change its prices or documents during this bid validity period.
  4058→
  4059→A bid has to be accompanied by a Bank Guarantee called the bid guarantee. This is a legal instrument for the customer which can be forfeited in the bank of the bidder in case (1) the bidder does not accept the bid corrections (as is discussed below) (2) the bidder withdraws its bid during the bid validity period. In such cases of withdrawal of bids, the bidder may be black listed for participating in future bids of the customer.
  4060→
  4061→4\. **Bid opening** – Once bids of various bidders are submitted, the opening of the bids can be on a single part or on a two part basis. In a single part bid, both the Prequalification bid and Price bid of all bidders are opened in front of representatives of all bidders and brief particulars submitted in the bids are announced including the Total Bid Price quoted. In a two part bid, only the Techno-commercial bid of all bidders are opened and brief particulars submitted in the bids are announced.
  4062→
  4063→5\. **Bid evaluation** – Evaluation of the bids is undertaken. Firstly, evaluation of Techno-Commercial Bids is undertaken to scrutiny if the submissions are in line with the requirements of the bidding documents. Bids which meet these requirements are called responsive Techno-commercial bids. Bidders submitting non responsive Techno-commercial bids are rejected and their Price bids are not evaluated though being announced on the bid opening date.
  4064→
  4065→Thereafter, in a single part bid, the Price bids are scrutinised for responsiveness for responsive Techno-commercial bidders only. Bidders submitting non responsive Price bids are rejected. Amongst the responsive Price bids of responsive Techno-commercial bidders, the least priced Bidder is declared as L1, the next L2 and so on. In a two part bid, the bidders who submitted responsive Techno-commercial bids are notified for the opening of their Price bids on a particular date and thereafter the process of Price bid evaluation and finalisation of L1, L2, etc. is undertaken in a similar manner as described above.
  4066→
  4067→A important note on Bid evaluation criteria. Most bids in India are based on least priced bids as described above. For a bid to be techno-commercial responsive, you just need to meet the minimum requirements stipulated in the specifications. All bidders who are responsive techno-commercially are treated equally. And then their Price bids are evaluated.
  4068→
  4069→However, in more stringent bids, many such in the international domain, the evaluation stipulates weightage of each bid. For example, a Techno-commercial bid may have a 60% weightage and the Price bid may have 40% weightage. In such a case, apart from meeting minimum requirements, the bidder whose technical and commercial particulars submitted are much of higher standards than others are rewarded by getting more points due to the high weightage. Even if the bid is not the least priced tender, the bidder may get the award if it scores high in the techno-commercial bid. **Such awards are not based on least priced bids.**
  4070→
  4071→6\. **Bid clarification** \- Small errors in the Techno-commercial bid and the Price bid can be corrected by the customer and notify the bidder for their acceptance. The customer can also seek for missing documents from the bidder. If a bidder cannot provide missing documents or do not accept to the logical corrections in the Price bid, the customer has the right to reject such bidders.
  4072→
  4073→6\. **Pre-award session** – The L1 bidder is invited for a pre-award session where the L1 bidder is asked to re-assure fulfilling the important conditions of the bidding documents. The contractor also confirms to provide required bank guarantees upon award and jointly with the customer draw up a item wise time lines of project execution. This broad timeline drawn in line with completion period of the project is called the L1 network. All these discussions are recorded to produce a Minutes of Meeting (MoU) signed off by both the customer and the L1 bidder.
  4074→
  4075→7\. **Letter of Intent (LoI**) – This step is not mandatory. Sometimes the customer proceeds directly to the next step. However, in some cases, the customer informs the L1 bidder of its intent to award the project. This is called the LoI which generally mentions the total contract price, if awarded.
  4076→
  4077→8\. **Notification of Award (NOA)** – The L1 bidder is invited and the customer places the award through a letter called the Notification of Award (NOA). NOA may also be called a Award, Order or a Contract. It is at this stage that a bid is called a Contract and the L1 bidder is now known as the Contractor. The NOA also comprises of the bidding documents, the Techno-commercial bid and the Price Bid, and the MoU of pre-award session.
  4078→
  4079→9\. **Project Execution** – Based on the terms and conditions laid out in the Contract, the project is executed by the contractor. On the basis of the L1 network, the contractor draws up a slightly detailed item wise timelines called the L2 network. The L2 network is discussed and approved by the customer. In some cases, further minutely detailed networks such as the L3, L4 and L5 networks are also drawn up.

**PART 28 : ANNEXURES**

Annexures deals with subjects which are sometimes wrongly interpreted or less understood. Therefore I thought to discuss on some subjects in this Part to provide clarity to young professionals. However, you can delve on these subjects in various documents for a deeper understanding.

**ANNEXURE: VISION & MISSION**

Many employees are not clear what a Mission or a Vision of an organization is. Let us try to understand.

Every organisation has a Mission and a Vision and they emphasize this to its employees and the outside world through a statement called the Vision and Mission statement.

A mission statement defines the organization's business, its objectives, and how it will reach these objectives. A vision statement details where the organization aspires to go.

Sometimes the terms “mission statement” and “vision statement” are used interchangeably or even combined into a single statement.  
But they mean two very different things. Your mission statement is what your company is doing right now, while your vision statement is what you hope to achieve in the future – where you are in this moment versus where you’re going.

Let me give a practical example. If someone asks you, “So, what do you do currently?” you might say, “I’m a Bidding Manager in a large-sized construction company”. But, what if they asked you, “What do you want to be in five or 10 years from now?” Your answer might be a bit different, right? Maybe you’d say, “My goal is to become the Strategic Business Head and ultimately would like to be the Managing Director of the organisation”

In the above example, as a Bidding Manager, your Mission is to win many bids and have the largest market share but your Vision may be to head the business and eventually become the MD of the organisation.

**ANNEXURE: DPR TO CONTRACTS CLOSURE**

There are various stages of a project executed by a customer through its selected contractor. Let us understand the entire process step by step.

1\. **DPR** \- Every customer studies in details the benefits of a project when implemented. It studies the requirements to build the project, ie. Land, cost of acquisition, permissions from different authorities, finance, manpower and equipment infrastructure and the timelines of construction. A Detail Project Report (DPR) is prepared comprising of above details which is subsequently approved by concerned authority.

2\. **NIT** \- The customer publishes in brief, in newspapers, websites, etc., about the requirement of the project and invites prospective contractors to participate in the bidding for the project. This is called the Notice Inviting Tender (NIT) which also mentions the last date of procuring the bidding documents.

3\. **Bid** \- Once the NIT is published, interested contractors purchase the bidding documents and prepare the bid. The last date of submission of the bid is also mentioned in the bidding documents. The bid generally has two parts, one is the Techno-commercial bid, which comprises of all technical and commercial documents required to be submitted in the bid, and the other is the Price or Financial bid which comprises of all Price Schedules detailing item wise and total prices with a Summary of Price Schedules. The compilation of all these documents is sealed in packets and submitted to the customer within the due date of submission is called the Bid. A bid needs to be valid for a period of time, could be three to six months, as prescribed. This means the bidder cannot change its prices or documents during this bid validity period.

A bid has to be accompanied by a Bank Guarantee called the bid guarantee. This is a legal instrument for the customer which can be forfeited in the bank of the bidder in case (1) the bidder does not accept the bid corrections (as is discussed below) (2) the bidder withdraws its bid during the bid validity period. In such cases of withdrawal of bids, the bidder may be black listed for participating in future bids of the customer.

4\. **Bid opening** – Once bids of various bidders are submitted, the opening of the bids can be on a single part or on a two part basis. In a single part bid, both the Prequalification bid and Price bid of all bidders are opened in front of representatives of all bidders and brief particulars submitted in the bids are announced including the Total Bid Price quoted. In a two part bid, only the Techno-commercial bid of all bidders are opened and brief particulars submitted in the bids are announced.

5\. **Bid evaluation** – Evaluation of the bids is undertaken. Firstly, evaluation of Techno-Commercial Bids is undertaken to scrutiny if the submissions are in line with the requirements of the bidding documents. Bids which meet these requirements are called responsive Techno-commercial bids. Bidders submitting non responsive Techno-commercial bids are rejected and their Price bids are not evaluated though being announced on the bid opening date.

Thereafter, in a single part bid, the Price bids are scrutinised for responsiveness for responsive Techno-commercial bidders only. Bidders submitting non responsive Price bids are rejected. Amongst the responsive Price bids of responsive Techno-commercial bidders, the least priced Bidder is declared as L1, the next L2 and so on. In a two part bid, the bidders who submitted responsive Techno-commercial bids are notified for the opening of their Price bids on a particular date and thereafter the process of Price bid evaluation and finalisation of L1, L2, etc. is undertaken in a similar manner as described above.

A important note on Bid evaluation criteria. Most bids in India are based on least priced bids as described above. For a bid to be techno-commercial responsive, you just need to meet the minimum requirements stipulated in the specifications. All bidders who are responsive techno-commercially are treated equally. And then their Price bids are evaluated.

However, in more stringent bids, many such in the international domain, the evaluation stipulates weightage of each bid. For example, a Techno-commercial bid may have a 60% weightage and the Price bid may have 40% weightage. In such a case, apart from meeting minimum requirements, the bidder whose technical and commercial particulars submitted are much of higher standards than others are rewarded by getting more points due to the high weightage. Even if the bid is not the least priced tender, the bidder may get the award if it scores high in the techno-commercial bid. **Such awards are not based on least priced bids.**

6\. **Bid clarification** \- Small errors in the Techno-commercial bid and the Price bid can be corrected by the customer and notify the bidder for their acceptance. The customer can also seek for missing documents from the bidder. If a bidder cannot provide missing documents or do not accept to the logical corrections in the Price bid, the customer has the right to reject such bidders.

6\. **Pre-award session** – The L1 bidder is invited for a pre-award session where the L1 bidder is asked to re-assure fulfilling the important conditions of the bidding documents. The contractor also confirms to provide required bank guarantees upon award and jointly with the customer draw up a item wise time lines of project execution. This broad timeline drawn in line with completion period of the project is called the L1 network. All these discussions are recorded to produce a Minutes of Meeting (MoU) signed off by both the customer and the L1 bidder.

7\. **Letter of Intent (LoI**) – This step is not mandatory. Sometimes the customer proceeds directly to the next step. However, in some cases, the customer informs the L1 bidder of its intent to award the project. This is called the LoI which generally mentions the total contract price, if awarded.

8\. **Notification of Award (NOA)** – The L1 bidder is invited and the customer places the award through a letter called the Notification of Award (NOA). NOA may also be called a Award, Order or a Contract. It is at this stage that a bid is called a Contract and the L1 bidder is now known as the Contractor. The NOA also comprises of the bidding documents, the Techno-commercial bid and the Price Bid, and the MoU of pre-award session.

9\. **Project Execution** – Based on the terms and conditions laid out in the Contract, the project is executed by the contractor. On the basis of the L1 network, the contractor draws up a slightly detailed item wise timelines called the L2 network. The L2 network is discussed and approved by the customer. In some cases, further minutely detailed networks such as the L3, L4 and L5 networks are also drawn up.

# Plan: Proofread and Edit "Avoid Misgovernance in Corporates"

## Context

The manuscript `original_draft.md` (4,721 lines, ~649KB) is a complete book draft by Abhik Pal about corporate governance. It needs to be:
1. Split into individual chapter markdown files
2. Committed at that state
3. Proofread and edited for spelling, grammar, phrasing, and sentence construction
4. Accompanied by a summary of all changes
5. Equipped with a Python markdown-to-PDF converter

## Step 1: Create the splitting script and split the manuscript

Create `split_manuscript.py` that reads `original_draft.md` and produces a `chapters/` directory tree.

**Directory structure** — two-level: section folders containing part folders containing chapter files:
```
chapters/
  00_frontmatter/
    01_introduction.md
    02_preface.md
    03_about_the_author.md
    04_acknowledgement.md
    05_disclaimer.md
  01_leadership/
    part_01_cxo_leadership/
      00_intro.md
      01_too_many_cxo_syndrome.md
      02_renowned_guy_syndrome.md
      ...
      99_good_practices.md
  02_people/
    part_02_employees/
      ...
  03_operations/
    part_03_strategy/
    part_04_marketing/
    ...
    part_15_safety/
  04_support_services/
    part_16_hr/
    ...
    part_26_csr/
  05_articles/
    part_27_new_concepts/
    part_28_annexures/
    part_29_personal_advice/
    part_30_my_learning/
```

**Splitting logic:**
- Section boundaries: `**SECTION N –` pattern
- Part boundaries: `**PART N**` (in body, after line ~424)
- Chapter boundaries: `**CHAPTER :` (109 occurrences) — standard chapters
- Annexure boundaries: `**ANNEXURE:` — Part 28 items
- Bold topic headers — Parts 29-30 topics (e.g., `**ATTITUDE**`, `**The Tongue**`)
- Good practices: `**SOME GOOD PRACTICES**` or `**Some good practices**`
- Intro text: content between part header and first chapter → `00_intro.md`

Each file gets YAML frontmatter recording original line range:
```yaml
---
title: "The 'Too Many CXO' Syndrome"
part: 1
section: 1
original_lines: "501-570"
---
```

Also generate `chapters/index.md` — a manifest listing all files in reading order.

**Estimated output:** ~170 files (6 frontmatter + ~109 chapters + ~15 annexures + ~19 advice topics + ~11 lessons + ~30 intros + ~23 good practices sections).

**Critical file:** `/Users/sagarpal/projects/baba_book/original_draft.md`

## Step 2: Git commit the split state

```bash
git add chapters/ split_manuscript.py
git commit -m "Split original_draft.md into individual chapter files"
```

## Step 3: Proofread and edit all chapters

**Strategy:** Dispatch parallel sub-agents, batched by Part (30 parts + frontmatter).

Each sub-agent receives:
- All chapter files for its assigned part(s)
- Editorial guidelines (below)
- Instructions to edit files in-place AND produce a structured change log

**Parallelism:** 5-7 sub-agents per wave, ~5 waves total. Smaller parts can be grouped (e.g., Parts 10-13 in one agent).

**Editorial guidelines for sub-agents:**
- Professional, authoritative tone (senior executive addressing management students)
- **Indian English** conventions (organisation, programme, honour, etc.)
- **Preserve** the author's voice, anecdotes, and personal style
- **Fix:** spelling errors, grammar, awkward phrasing, sentence construction, punctuation, subject-verb agreement, tense consistency
- **Do NOT change:** domain terms (SBU, BU, CXO, DPR, NIT, LoI, NOA, etc.), Indian business terms, currency (Rs, Cr, Lakhs)
- **Do NOT** add or remove content — only improve existing text
- Each chapter's "The salient points are:" section should be checked for consistency

**Change log format** — each sub-agent returns a structured list:
```
| Location | Type | Original | Edited | Reason |
```

## Step 4: Create EDIT_SUMMARY.md

Consolidate all change logs from sub-agents into a single `EDIT_SUMMARY.md` organized by Section → Part → Chapter, with references to original line numbers (from YAML frontmatter).

## Step 5: Create `generate_pdf.py`

A Python script using `markdown-it-py` + `weasyprint` (or `markdown2` + `weasyprint`) to:
1. Read `chapters/index.md` for file ordering
2. Convert each markdown file to HTML
3. Apply a book-appropriate CSS stylesheet (A4, serif font, proper margins, page breaks between chapters, page numbers)
4. Render to a single PDF

Include `requirements.txt` with dependencies.

## Step 6: Final commits

```bash
git add chapters/ EDIT_SUMMARY.md
git commit -m "Proofread and edit all chapters"

git add generate_pdf.py requirements.txt
git commit -m "Add markdown-to-PDF conversion script"
```

## Verification

1. After split: verify no content lost by comparing concatenated chapters against original
2. After edits: spot-check several chapters from different sections
3. PDF script: run `python generate_pdf.py` and verify output renders correctly

## Context

The manuscript `original_draft.md` (4,721 lines, ~649KB) is a complete book draft by Abhik Pal about corporate governance. It needs to be:
1. Split into individual chapter markdown files
2. Committed at that state
3. Proofread and edited for spelling, grammar, phrasing, and sentence construction

## Step 1: Create the splitting script and split the manuscript

Create `split_manuscript.py` that reads `original_draft.md` and produces a `chapters/` directory tree.

**Directory structure** — two-level: section folders containing part folders containing chapter files:
```

  00_frontmatter/

      01_too_many_cxo_syndrome.md
      02_renowned_guy_syndrome.md
      ...
      99_good_practices.md
  02_people/
    part_02_employees/
      ...
  03_operations/
    part_03_strategy/
    part_04_marketing/
    ...
    part_15_safety/
  04_support_services/
    part_16_hr/
    ...
    part_26_csr/
  05_articles/
    part_27_new_concepts/
    part_28_annexures/
    part_29_personal_advice/
    part_30_my_learning/
```

**Splitting logic:**
- Section boundaries: `**SECTION N –` pattern
- Part boundaries: `**PART N**` (in body, after line ~424)
- Chapter boundaries: `**CHAPTER :` (109 occurrences) — standard chapters
- Annexure boundaries: `**ANNEXURE:` — Part 28 items
- Bold topic headers — Parts 29-30 topics (e.g., `**ATTITUDE**`, `**The Tongue**`)
- Good practices: `**SOME GOOD PRACTICES**` or `**Some good practices**`
- Intro text: content between part header and first chapter → `00_intro.md`

Each file gets YAML frontmatter recording original line range:
```yaml
---
title: "The 'Too Many CXO' Syndrome"
part: 1
section: 1
original_lines: "501-570"
---
```

Also generate `chapters/index.md` — a manifest listing all files in reading order.

**Estimated output:** ~170 files (6 frontmatter + ~109 chapters + ~15 annexures + ~19 advice topics + ~11 lessons + ~30 intros + ~23 good practices sections).

**Critical file:** `/Users/sagarpal/projects/baba_book/original_draft.md`

## Step 2: Git commit the split state

```bash
git add chapters/ split_manuscript.py
git commit -m "Split original_draft.md into individual chapter files"
```

## Step 3: Proofread and edit all chapters

**Strategy:** Dispatch parallel sub-agents, batched by Part (30 parts + frontmatter).

Each sub-agent receives:
- All chapter files for its assigned part(s)
- Editorial guidelines (below)
- Instructions to edit files in-place AND produce a structured change log

**Parallelism:** 5-7 sub-agents per wave, ~5 waves total. Smaller parts can be grouped (e.g., Parts 10-13 in one agent).

**Editorial guidelines for sub-agents:**
- Professional, authoritative tone (senior executive addressing management students)
- **Indian English** conventions (organisation, programme, honour, etc.)
- **Preserve** the author's voice, anecdotes, and personal style
- **Fix:** spelling errors, grammar, awkward phrasing, sentence construction, punctuation, subject-verb agreement, tense consistency
- **Do NOT change:** domain terms (SBU, BU, CXO, DPR, NIT, LoI, NOA, etc.), Indian business terms, currency (Rs, Cr, Lakhs)
- **Do NOT** add or remove content — only improve existing text
- Each chapter's "The salient points are:" section should be checked for consistency

**Change log format** — each sub-agent returns a structured list:
```
| Location | Type | Original | Edited | Reason |
```

## Step 4: Create EDIT_SUMMARY.md

Consolidate all change logs from sub-agents into a single `EDIT_SUMMARY.md` organized by Section → Part → Chapter, with references to original line numbers (from YAML frontmatter).

## Step 5: Create `generate_pdf.py`

A Python script using `markdown-it-py` + `weasyprint` (or `markdown2` + `weasyprint`) to:
1. Read `chapters/index.md` for file ordering
2. Convert each markdown file to HTML
3. Apply a book-appropriate CSS stylesheet (A4, serif font, proper margins, page breaks between chapters, page numbers)
4. Render to a single PDF

Include `requirements.txt` with dependencies.

## Step 6: Final commits

```bash
git add chapters/ EDIT_SUMMARY.md
git commit -m "Proofread and edit all chapters"

git add generate_pdf.py requirements.txt
git commit -m "Add markdown-to-PDF conversion script"
```

## Verification

1. After split: verify no content lost by comparing concatenated chapters against original
2. After edits: spot-check several chapters from different sections
3. PDF script: run `python generate_pdf.py` and verify output renders correctly

# Plan: Proofread and Edit "Avoid Misgovernance in Corporates"

## Context

The manuscript `original_draft.md` (4,721 lines, ~649KB) is a complete book draft by Abhik Pal about corporate governance. It needs to be:
1. Split into individual chapter markdown files
2. Committed at that state
3. Proofread and edited for spelling, grammar, phrasing, and sentence construction
4. Accompanied by a summary of all changes
5. Equipped with a Python markdown-to-PDF converter

## Step 1: Create the splitting script and split the manuscript

Create `split_manuscript.py` that reads `original_draft.md` and produces a `chapters/` directory tree.

**Directory structure** — two-level: section folders containing part folders containing chapter files:
```
chapters/
  00_frontmatter/
    01_introduction.md
    02_preface.md
    03_about_the_author.md
    04_acknowledgement.md
    05_disclaimer.md
  01_leadership/
    part_01_cxo_leadership/
      00_intro.md
      01_too_many_cxo_syndrome.md
      02_renowned_guy_syndrome.md
      ...
      99_good_practices.md
  02_people/
    part_02_employees/
      ...
  03_operations/
    part_03_strategy/
    part_04_marketing/
    ...
    part_15_safety/
  04_support_services/
    part_16_hr/
    ...
    part_26_csr/
  05_articles/
    part_27_new_concepts/
    part_28_annexures/
    part_29_personal_advice/
    part_30_my_learning/
```

**Splitting logic:**
- Section boundaries: `**SECTION N –` pattern
- Part boundaries: `**PART N**` (in body, after line ~424)
- Chapter boundaries: `**CHAPTER :` (109 occurrences) — standard chapters
- Annexure boundaries: `**ANNEXURE:` — Part 28 items
- Bold topic headers — Parts 29-30 topics (e.g., `**ATTITUDE**`, `**The Tongue**`)
- Good practices: `**SOME GOOD PRACTICES**` or `**Some good practices**`
- Intro text: content between part header and first chapter → `00_intro.md`

Each file gets YAML frontmatter recording original line range:
```yaml
---
title: "The 'Too Many CXO' Syndrome"
part: 1
section: 1
original_lines: "501-570"
---
```

Also generate `chapters/index.md` — a manifest listing all files in reading order.

**Estimated output:** ~170 files (6 frontmatter + ~109 chapters + ~15 annexures + ~19 advice topics + ~11 lessons + ~30 intros + ~23 good practices sections).

**Critical file:** `/Users/sagarpal/projects/baba_book/original_draft.md`

## Step 2: Git commit the split state

```bash
git add chapters/ split_manuscript.py
git commit -m "Split original_draft.md into individual chapter files"
```

## Step 3: Proofread and edit all chapters

**Strategy:** Dispatch parallel sub-agents, batched by Part (30 parts + frontmatter).

Each sub-agent receives:
- All chapter files for its assigned part(s)
- Editorial guidelines (below)
- Instructions to edit files in-place AND produce a structured change log

**Parallelism:** 5-7 sub-agents per wave, ~5 waves total. Smaller parts can be grouped (e.g., Parts 10-13 in one agent).

**Editorial guidelines for sub-agents:**
- Professional, authoritative tone (senior executive addressing management students)
- **Indian English** conventions (organisation, programme, honour, etc.)
- **Preserve** the author's voice, anecdotes, and personal style
- **Fix:** spelling errors, grammar, awkward phrasing, sentence construction, punctuation, subject-verb agreement, tense consistency
- **Do NOT change:** domain terms (SBU, BU, CXO, DPR, NIT, LoI, NOA, etc.), Indian business terms, currency (Rs, Cr, Lakhs)
- **Do NOT** add or remove content — only improve existing text
- Each chapter's "The salient points are:" section should be checked for consistency

**Change log format** — each sub-agent returns a structured list:
```
| Location | Type | Original | Edited | Reason |
```

## Step 4: Create EDIT_SUMMARY.md

Consolidate all change logs from sub-agents into a single `EDIT_SUMMARY.md` organized by Section → Part → Chapter, with references to original line numbers (from YAML frontmatter).

## Step 5: Create `generate_pdf.py`

A Python script using `markdown-it-py` + `weasyprint` (or `markdown2` + `weasyprint`) to:
1. Read `chapters/index.md` for file ordering
2. Convert each markdown file to HTML
3. Apply a book-appropriate CSS stylesheet (A4, serif font, proper margins, page breaks between chapters, page numbers)
4. Render to a single PDF

Include `pyproject.toml` with `uv`-compatible dependency configuration.

## Step 6: Final commits

```bash
git add chapters/ EDIT_SUMMARY.md
git commit -m "Proofread and edit all chapters"

git add generate_pdf.py requirements.txt
git commit -m "Add markdown-to-PDF conversion script"
```

## Verification

1. After split: verify no content lost by comparing concatenated chapters against original
2. After edits: spot-check several chapters from different sections
3. PDF script: run `python generate_pdf.py` and verify output renders correctly

lines: git commit -m "Proofread and edit all chapters"
